title: Acne Vulgaris: Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Epidemiology and Clinical Impact

Acne vulgaris affects approximately 85% of adolescents aged 12-24 years, with peak incidence during teenage years. The condition persists into adulthood in 12-14% of adults, particularly women. Annual healthcare costs exceed $1.2 billion in the United States alone.

Pathophysiology of Acne Vulgaris

Acne develops through four interconnected pathophysiologic mechanisms: (1) increased sebum production stimulated by androgens, (2) follicular hyperkeratinization causing comedone formation, (3) bacterial colonization by Cutibacterium acnes, and (4) innate and adaptive immune-mediated inflammation. Androgens, particularly dihydrotestosterone (DHT), increase sebaceous gland size and sebum production. The bacterium's lipopolysaccharide activates toll-like receptors, triggering IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α production.

Clinical Presentation and Morphology

Acne presents with distinctive lesion types: comedones (blackheads and whiteheads from follicular obstruction), inflammatory papules and pustules (1-5mm raised lesions), nodules (>5mm deeper lesions), and cysts (fluid-filled nodules). Lesions predominantly appear on the face, chest, shoulders, and back where sebaceous gland density is highest.

First-Line Treatment Approaches

Topical Retinoids: Tretinoin (0.025-0.1%), adapalene (0.1%), normalize follicular keratinization. Start with lowest concentrations applied 3 times weekly, increasing to nightly use.

Benzoyl Peroxide: 2.5%, 5%, and 10% formulations provide bactericidal activity. Effective monotherapy for mild acne; combines synergistically with topical antibiotics. Use 1-2 times daily.

Combination Therapy: Benzoyl peroxide (5%) with clindamycin 1% twice daily is superior to either agent alone. Adapalene 0.3% plus benzoyl peroxide 2.5% (Epiduo) reduces resistance and improves compliance.

Oral Antibiotic Therapy

Oral antibiotics address inflammatory acne through bacterial suppression and anti-inflammatory effects. Doxycycline (100mg daily) and minocycline (50-100mg daily) are first-line choices due to superior skin penetration and anti-inflammatory properties. Azithromycin (250mg 3 times weekly) provides alternatives for doxycycline-intolerant patients. Always combine oral antibiotics with benzoyl peroxide to prevent resistance; maximum duration is 12 weeks.

Hormonal Therapy

Oral contraceptives containing ethinyl estradiol with norgestimate, norethindrone, or drospirenone reduce androgens and sebum production. Norgestimate-containing pills show 31-35% acne improvement versus placebo. Spironolactone (50-200mg daily) effectively treats inflammatory acne in women by blocking androgen receptors on sebaceous glands.

Isotretinoin Therapy

Isotretinoin (0.5-1.0mg/kg/day, cumulative dose target 120-150mg/kg) is curative for severe nodular acne. Approximately 90% of patients achieve long-term remission or cure. Requires iPLEDGE registration, monthly pregnancy tests for females, and baseline lipid/liver function testing. Common side effects include cheilitis (100%), xerosis (80%), and arthralgias (25%).

Prevention and Lifestyle Management

Gentle twice-daily facial cleansing with non-comedogenic cleansers minimizes irritation. Non-comedogenic moisturizers and sunscreens (SPF 30+) address dryness from retinoids. Avoid excessive facial manipulation and oil-based cosmetics.

References

  1. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
  2. Dreno B. Epidemiology of acne. Acta Dermatol Venereol. 2021;101(10):adv00558.
  3. Chu A. Revisiting the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris. J Clin Aesthet Dermatol. 2017;10(11):35-40.
  4. Webster GF. Pathophysiology of acne. Cutis. 2005;76(1 Suppl):4-7.
  5. Thiboutot D, et al. New insights into the management of acne. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2009;60(5):S1-S50.
  6. Cunliffe WJ. Acne and rosacea in clinical practice. Martin Dunitz. 1998.
  7. Shalita AR, et al. Topical nicotinamide compared with clindamycin gel. Int J Dermatol. 1995;34(6):434-437.
  8. Lehmann HP, et al. Isotretinoin and risk of depression: A systematic review. Dermatol Online J. 2010;16(3):10.
meta_description: Acne Vulgaris affects 85% of teens. Learn about pathophysiology, treatment with isotretinoin, antibiotics, and retinoids from dermatology experts.

title: Cystic Acne: A Comprehensive Clinical Guide
content:
Definition and Clinical Features

Cystic acne represents the most severe form of acne vulgaris, characterized by large (>5mm), painful, indurated nodules and cysts deep within the dermis. These lesions form when the follicular rupture extends deeply into dermal tissue, triggering intense inflammatory responses. Unlike superficial papules and pustules, cystic lesions involve subcutaneous abscesses with significant scarring potential.

Epidemiology and Risk Stratification

Cystic acne affects 1-2% of adolescent populations, more commonly males (10:1 ratio) due to androgen sensitivity. Family history is strongly predictive; 60% of patients report positive family history of severe acne. Risk factors include male gender, delayed treatment initiation, and genetic predisposition to heightened innate immune responses.

Pathophysiology of Severe Acne

Cystic acne develops when follicular rupture occurs deep within the dermis. The follicular wall ruptures due to intense inflammatory pressure from accumulated keratin, sebum, and bacteria. Subsequent spillage of follicular contents into surrounding dermis triggers foreign body reactions, abscess formation, and intense neutrophilic infiltration. IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and IL-17 levels are dramatically elevated compared to mild-moderate acne, driving deeper inflammation and tissue necrosis.

Clinical Presentation

Lesions present as large, firm nodules without pustular heads, often appearing purple or flesh-colored. They localize to the face, neck, chest, shoulders, and back. Pain is characteristic; cystic lesions are tender to palpation due to deep dermal involvement. Multiple lesions may coalesce into larger inflammatory masses. Patients report significant psychosocial burden, depression, and quality-of-life impairment.

Diagnostic Evaluation

Diagnosis is clinical; bacterial culture is unnecessary except when secondary infection is suspected. Assessment should include GAGS scoring, photographic documentation of lesion count and distribution, and baseline evaluation for systemic findings (hirsutism, irregular menses suggesting hormonal dysfunction). Serum androgens, DHEA-S, and free testosterone may be indicated in women with severe acne, early-onset acne, or features suggesting PCOS or androgen excess.

Isotretinoin: The Definitive Treatment

Indication and Efficacy: Isotretinoin is the gold standard for cystic acne, with 90% of patients achieving long-term remission or complete cure. It is indicated for: (1) severe nodular acne with scarring potential, (2) moderate acne unresponsive to maximal conventional therapy (oral antibiotics + topical retinoids for 3+ months), (3) acne with significant psychosocial impact, and (4) rapid disease progression.

Mechanism: Isotretinoin is a vitamin A derivative that suppresses sebaceous gland activity, reducing sebum production by 90%. It normalizes follicular keratinization, has direct anti-inflammatory effects, and possesses antibacterial properties. Dosing starts at 0.5mg/kg/day, escalating to 1.0mg/kg/day as tolerated. Target cumulative dose is 120-150mg/kg for disease cure.

Treatment Course: Standard treatment lasts 15-20 weeks. At cumulative doses of 120mg/kg, clearance rates approach 95%. Patients taking suboptimal cumulative doses experience significantly higher relapse rates.

Isotretinoin: Safety and Monitoring

Teratogenicity: Isotretinoin is highly teratogenic (major malformations in 25% of exposed pregnancies). It is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Women of reproductive potential require two forms of contraception, monthly pregnancy tests (25mIU/mL sensitivity), and iPLEDGE program enrollment. Male patients require monthly iPLEDGE authorization.

Baseline Laboratory Assessment: Obtain fasting lipid panel and liver function tests (AST, ALT). Repeat monthly during treatment. Hypertriglyceridemia (>400mg/dL) and hepatic transaminitis (>3x upper limit normal) require dose reduction or discontinuation.

Mucocutaneous Side Effects: Cheilitis (severe lip dryness) affects 100% of patients; manage with emollients and lip balm. Xerosis (skin dryness) occurs in 80%; use fragrance-free moisturizers. Conjunctivitis and dry eyes affect 40%; monitor for contact lens intolerance.

Systemic Side Effects: Myalgias and arthralgias (25%) are usually manageable. Headaches (20%) rarely require intervention. Night vision deterioration is rare but warrants ophthalmologic referral. Inflammatory bowel disease risk is debated; counsel patients regarding abdominal symptoms.

Alternative Treatments Before Isotretinoin

If isotretinoin is declined or contraindicated, aggressive combination therapy may delay progression: (1) Oral isotretinoin-strength antibiotics (doxycycline 100mg twice daily) combined with (2) topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.1% nightly) plus (3) benzoyl peroxide 5% (morning) or (4) hormonal therapy in women (oral contraceptive with androgen-blocking progestin or spironolactone 100-200mg daily).

Intralesional Steroid Injections

Triamcinolone acetonide 2.5-5mg/mL injected into individual cystic lesions rapidly reduces inflammation and prevents rupture. Injections are repeated every 4-6 weeks. Intralesional steroids are useful as adjunctive therapy while awaiting isotretinoin initiation or for isolated cysts in otherwise controlled patients. Atrophy risk is minimized with proper dilution and injection technique.

Psychosocial Impact and Patient Support

Severe acne significantly impacts mental health; 64% of patients with severe acne report depression, anxiety, or suicidal ideation. Dermatologists should assess psychological burden at each visit and refer for mental health support when indicated. Emphasizing realistic timelines (6-8 weeks for improvement with conventional therapy; 15-20 weeks for isotretinoin) and setting appropriate expectations improves satisfaction.

Post-Treatment Management and Scar Revision

After achieving clearance on isotretinoin, maintenance therapy is typically unnecessary; relapse is uncommon. However, residual atrophic scars develop in 12-30% of patients with severe cystic acne. Early isotretinoin initiation minimizes scarring. Established atrophic scars respond to subcision, punch excision, laser resurfacing (fractional CO2 or erbium), or microneedling performed 6-12 months after isotretinoin completion.

References

  1. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
  2. Rodondi A, et al. Severe nodulocystic acne: Clinical and therapeutic approach. J Clin Med. 2022;11(17):5240.
  3. Dreno B, et al. Pathophysiology of acne: Targets and therapeutics. J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2022;36(1):2-16.
  4. Hung SJ, et al. Isotretinoin use and risk of psychiatric adverse effects: A systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2017;77(6):1068-1079.
  5. Strauss JS, et al. Consensus Conference on Isotretinoin. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2001;45(2):S146-S154.
  6. Magin P, et al. Systematic review of the epidemiology of acne vulgaris. J Prim Health Care. 2011;3(1):37-46.
  7. Goulden V, et al. Post-adolescent acne. Int J Dermatol. 1997;36(6):441-446.
  8. Bhate K, Williams HC. Epidemiology of acne vulgaris. Br J Dermatol. 2013;168(3):474-485.
meta_description: Cystic acne treatment: isotretinoin 0.5-1mg/kg cumulative 120-150mg/kg is curative. Learn about severe nodular acne management with dermatology experts.

title: Hormonal Acne: A Comprehensive Clinical Guide
content:
Definition and Clinical Significance

Hormonal acne is acne predominantly triggered or worsened by androgen fluctuations, occurring most commonly in women during reproductive years. It represents 40-50% of acne cases in adult women versus 15% in men. Adult-onset acne in women is almost always hormonally mediated, distinguishing it from adolescent acne which has multifactorial etiology.

Epidemiology and Demographics

Hormonal acne predominantly affects women aged 20-40 years. Prevalence increases in the third and fourth decades of life, opposite the pattern in males. Population studies show 15-20% of adult women experience clinically significant acne, with 35% of women aged 40-49 reporting acne symptoms. Female-to-male ratio for adult acne is approximately 2.5:1.

Pathophysiology of Androgenic Acne

Hormonal acne develops through androgen-driven sebaceous gland dysfunction. The mechanism involves: (1) increased serum androgens (elevated DHEA-S, free testosterone, or androstenedione), (2) elevated tissue-level androgen receptor expression in sebaceous glands and hair follicles, (3) increased 5-alpha reductase activity converting testosterone to DHT (more potent), and (4) heightened sebaceous gland sensitivity to androgens.

In women, sources of excess androgens include polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS, 20-30% of women with acne), adrenal hyperplasia (1-3%), ovarian androgen-producing tumors (rare), and iatrogenic causes (anabolic steroids, progestin-only contraceptives with androgenic activity). Luteal phase elevation in progesterone and androgen levels creates cyclical flares in susceptible women.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Hormonal acne has distinctive features: deep nodular and cystic lesions on the lower face, jawline, and chin (androgen-rich areas); cyclical flares aligned with menstrual cycle (commonly 7-10 days before menses); predominantly inflammatory lesions rather than comedones; and tendency toward persistence beyond teenage years into adulthood. Acne often worsens with hormonal fluctuations (pregnancy, postpartum period, perimenopause).

Diagnostic Evaluation

Clinical diagnosis relies on characteristic distribution (jawline, chin, lower face) and cyclical pattern. Laboratory testing is indicated in women with: early-onset severe acne, irregular or absent menses, hirsutism, alopecia, or clitoromegaly. Order serum free testosterone, total testosterone, DHEA-S, and LH/FSH ratio (fasting morning specimen). PCOS is suggested by LH:FSH ratio >3:1 and elevated androgens. Pelvic ultrasound assesses for polycystic ovarian morphology if PCOS is suspected.

Oral Contraceptive Therapy

Mechanism: Oral contraceptives (OCPs) reduce acne through multiple mechanisms: estrogen increases sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), which binds circulating androgens and reduces free (biologically active) testosterone. Progestins with low androgenic activity directly suppress LH, reducing ovarian androgen production.

FDA-Approved Options: Triphasic norgestimate/EE (30mcg) (Ortho Tri-Cyclen, others), norethindrone/EE (35mcg), and drospirenone/EE (20mcg) (Yaz, others) are FDA-approved for acne. Clinical trials demonstrate 32-35% acne improvement versus placebo. Norgestimate-containing pills show best efficacy for acne; drospirenone has anti-androgenic properties through mild aldosterone antagonism.

Treatment Timeline: Improvement requires 3-6 months due to menstrual cycling effects; full benefit emerges at 6-12 months. OCPs with lower estrogen doses are preferred in modern practice to minimize thromboembolism risk while maintaining acne efficacy.

Spironolactone Therapy

Mechanism and Dosing: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic that functions as an aldosterone antagonist and androgen receptor antagonist. It blocks androgen effects in sebaceous glands and reduces ovarian androgen production by suppressing LH. Standard dosing for acne is 50-200mg daily (typically 100-200mg in divided doses). Clinical improvement requires 3-6 months of therapy.

Efficacy: 60-90% of women experience 50% improvement in inflammatory acne with spironolactone monotherapy. Response is superior when combined with retinoids or benzoyl peroxide. Maximum benefit occurs at cumulative doses of 100-200mg daily.

Monitoring and Safety: Baseline and monthly serum potassium and creatinine monitoring is essential; spironolactone increases hyperkalemia risk (particularly in renal impairment or when combined with ACE inhibitors). Monitoring intervals can extend to every 3-6 months after initial stabilization. Absolute contraindications include baseline serum potassium >5.0 mEq/L, significant renal impairment (eGFR <30), or pregnancy (teratogenic).

Common Side Effects: Dysfunctional uterine bleeding or amenorrhea occurs in 20-30% of patients (often beneficial when combined with OCPs). Menstrual changes typically resolve within 3 months. Breast tenderness (10-15%) usually diminishes with continued therapy. Dizziness from volume depletion occurs in 5-10%.

Combination Hormonal Therapy

Superior acne control is achieved combining oral contraceptive (for SHBG elevation and LH suppression) with spironolactone (for androgen receptor blockade). This combination targets complementary mechanisms: OCP addresses central hormonal suppression while spironolactone blocks peripheral androgen effects. Response rates exceed 70% with combination therapy versus 50-60% for monotherapy.

Topical Adjunctive Therapy

Systemic hormonal therapy should be combined with topical treatments for additive benefit. Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025-0.1% or adapalene 0.1%) normalize follicular keratinization and provide anti-inflammatory effects. Benzoyl peroxide 5% combined with topical clindamycin provides antibacterial activity. Combination regimens accelerate improvement; typical timeline is 8-12 weeks to visible response.

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) Considerations

PCOS affects 8-13% of reproductive-age women and causes acne in 20-40% of PCOS patients. Acne in PCOS typically presents with severe nodular lesions on lower face due to androgen excess. Treatment addresses both dermatologic and metabolic manifestations: metformin (1500-2000mg daily) improves ovulatory function and reduces androgens; oral contraceptives suppress LH; spironolactone blocks peripheral androgen effects. Inositol supplementation (myo-inositol 2-4g daily with D-chiro-inositol) improves metabolic and hormonal parameters in PCOS.

Pregnancy and Postpartum Acne

Pregnancy causes acne in 40-50% of women due to elevated androgen metabolites and progesterone. Isotretinoin is absolutely contraindicated; safe options include topical azelaic acid (15-20%), topical clindamycin, and erythromycin (oral or topical, though minimally effective). Postpartum acne frequently occurs 3-6 months after delivery due to hormonal shifts and can be managed with topical retinoids, oral antibiotics, or hormonal therapy in non-breastfeeding mothers.

Perimenopause and Menopausal Acne

Acne may intensify or emerge during perimenopause (ages 40-55) due to fluctuating estrogen and progesterone with relatively stable androgens. OCPs (low-dose estrogen) are contraindicated in women >35 who smoke or have additional cardiovascular risk factors. Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) with estrogen-dominant formulations may improve acne. Topical and systemic antibiotics combined with retinoids provide safer alternatives than systemic hormonal therapy.

References

  1. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
  2. Ottander U, et al. Spironolactone for female acne: Evidence for efficacy and safety. Acta Derm Venereol. 2021;101(1):adv00407.
  3. Thorneycroft IH. Hormonal causes and treatment of acne in women. Int J Dermatol. 1998;37(6):426-432.
  4. Lucky AW, et al. Effectiveness of norgestimate and ethinyl estradiol in treating moderate acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1997;37(5):746-754.
  5. Poli F, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of acne. J Drugs Dermatol. 2019;18(1):62-69.
  6. Leyden JJ. Hormonal effects on skin physiology in women. Cutis. 2001;68(2 Suppl):14-18.
  7. Tan JK, et al. Global evidence-based guidelines on the management of acne. J Cosmet Dermatol. 2020;19(10):2596-2609.
meta_description: Hormonal acne in women: spironolactone 50-200mg, oral contraceptives, PCOS screening. Expert dermatology guide to androgen-driven acne.

title: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis): Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Epidemiology and Disease Burden

Atopic dermatitis (AD) is the most common inflammatory skin disease globally, affecting 15-20% of children and 1-3% of adults. Prevalence has tripled since the 1970s, particularly in developed nations. AD causes substantial morbidity: 60% of patients with moderate-severe disease experience sleep disruption; 30% report moderate-to-severe impact on quality of life. Annual healthcare costs exceed $5.3 billion in the United States.

Pathophysiology of Atopic Dermatitis

AD results from complex interaction between genetic predisposition, skin barrier dysfunction, and immune dysregulation. Genetic factors: Loss-of-function mutations in the filaggrin gene (FLG) occur in 10-15% of AD patients. Filaggrin is essential for stratum corneum formation; reduced expression impairs barrier integrity. Barrier dysfunction: Reduced ceramide content (25-50% reduction), abnormal lipid architecture, and elevated transepidermal water loss (TEWL) characterize AD skin.

Immune dysregulation: Type 2 immune activation predominates (Th2, Th22 cells produce IL-4, IL-13, IL-31). IL-4 and IL-13 signaling through the IL-4 receptor promotes IgE production (50-80% of AD patients are atopic with elevated serum IgE), triggers B cell differentiation, and suppresses filaggrin expression. IL-31 from cutaneous lymphocytes directly activates itch receptors on sensory nerves, explaining intense pruritus characteristic of AD.

Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis

AD presents with intense pruritus (itch-scratch cycle is cardinal feature), xerosis (excessive dryness), and acute/chronic inflammation with variable morphology. Acute lesions are erythematous, edematous, vesicular, and oozing. Chronic lesions show lichenification (thickened, cross-hatched skin) from repeated scratching. Distribution varies by age: infants and young children show facial and extensor involvement; older children and adults show flexural distribution (antecubital and popliteal fossae, neck, hands).

Diagnosis uses the Hanifin and Rajka criteria or UK diagnostic criteria. Essential features include early onset, intense pruritus, and chronic relapsing course. Supporting features include atopic personal/family history (asthma, allergic rhinitis, food allergy), elevated serum IgE, and positive skin prick tests. Diagnosis is clinical; no specific diagnostic test exists.

AD Severity Assessment

Severity classification guides treatment intensity: Mild: Limited lesions affecting <10% body surface area (BSA), minimal impact on quality of life. Moderate: 10-50% BSA involvement or significant functional impairment despite localized disease. Severe: >50% BSA involvement or severe impact on sleep and daily activities despite maximal topical therapy. The EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index) and IGA (Investigator's Global Assessment) quantify disease severity objectively.

First-Line Therapy: Emollients and Barrier Repair

Ceramide-Based Moisturizers: The cornerstone of AD management is liberal use of ceramide-containing emollients (creams and ointments preferred over lotions). Products containing ceramide 1, 3, and 6-II in optimal ratios (CeraVe, Aveeno Eczema Therapy, Eucerin Advanced Cleansing) restore skin barrier integrity. Patients should apply emollients twice daily within 3 minutes of bathing to occlude moisture. Studies show ceramide-based products reduce corticosteroid requirement by 30-50%.

Bathing Modifications: Lukewarm (not hot) water for 5-10 minutes daily followed by immediate emollient application is optimal. Avoid excessive cleansing; gentle, fragrance-free cleansers are preferable. Frequent baths remove natural oils despite subsequent moisturizing, so 2-3 times weekly is reasonable for severe disease.

Topical Corticosteroids

Potency Classes and Dosing: Topical corticosteroids (TCS) are first-line anti-inflammatory therapy. Low-potency agents (hydrocortisone 1%, desonide 0.05%) treat face and intertriginous areas. Mid-potency agents (triamcinolone 0.1%, fluticasone propionate 0.005%) treat trunk and extremities in children; high-potency agents (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) are reserved for adults with severe disease limited to non-facial areas.

Application and Efficacy: Apply TCS to active inflammation twice daily; taper when disease improves. Fingertip unit dosing (0.5g covers approximately 2 hand areas) ensures appropriate quantity. Approximately 70% of mild-moderate AD improves with optimized TCS and emollients alone.

Safety Considerations: Long-term TCS use carries risk of skin atrophy (1-2% with mid-potency, higher with superpotent agents). Monitor for systemic absorption in young children or with large surface area application (>20% BSA). Intermittent TCS (weekends only) can maintain remission in some patients.

Topical Calcineurin Inhibitors

Tacrolimus (0.03% and 0.1% ointment) and pimecrolimus (1% cream) are non-steroidal immunosuppressants that inhibit T cell activation. They are particularly useful for: face and intertriginous areas (avoiding TCS-induced atrophy), patients concerned about steroid side effects, and those requiring long-term control. Tacrolimus 0.1% is comparable to mid-potency TCS in efficacy; clinical response requires 3-4 weeks. A black box warning (2006) regarding theoretical lymphoma risk has not been validated in clinical practice; >20 years of post-marketing data support safety.

PDE4 Inhibitors

Crisaborole (ointment 2%) is a phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4) inhibitor that reduces inflammatory cytokine production. It is effective for mild-moderate AD and is FDA-approved for ages 2 and older. Crisaborole lacks steroid and calcineurin inhibitor side effects but is generally less potent than mid-potency TCS. Burning sensation during application occurs in 15-25% of patients, limiting palatability.

Systemic Biologic Therapy

Dupilumab (Dupixent): A monoclonal antibody targeting IL-4 receptor alpha (blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling). Dupilumab 300mg subcutaneously every 2 weeks (after 600mg loading dose) produces 40-50% improvement in EASI in 70-75% of moderate-severe AD patients by week 16. Approximately 35% achieve complete/near-complete clearance. FDA-approved for ages 6 months and older.

Tralokinumab (Adtralza): An IL-13-specific monoclonal antibody given as 600mg loading dose, then 300mg every 2 weeks. Clinical trials show EASI-75 response (75% improvement) in 45-50% of patients; efficacy is slightly lower than dupilumab but represents a valuable alternative.

Baricitinib (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor): A small molecule JAK inhibitor (2mg daily) FDA-approved for moderate-severe AD in adults. Approximately 65% of patients achieve EASI-75 response. Requires monitoring for infections, lipid elevation, and creatinine elevation.

Biologics Selection: Dupilumab is typically first-line for patients with baseline elevated serum IgE or eosinophilia (>400 cells/mcL). Tralokinumab is preferred if IL-4 elevation is suspected. Baricitinib provides rapid onset (3-4 weeks) compared to biologics (8-12 weeks). Switching between biologics is reasonable if inadequate response at 16-20 weeks.

Phototherapy

Narrow-band UVB (NB-UVB) phototherapy (311nm wavelength, 2-3 times weekly) is effective for generalized AD (>10% BSA). Response rates are 60-70% with improvement visible at 8-12 weeks. Phototherapy is particularly valuable during pregnancy when systemic therapies are limited. PUVA (psoralen + UVA) is less safe in children due to carcinogenicity risk.

Managing Acute Flares and Infection

Acute flares are managed with short courses of higher-potency TCS or systemic corticosteroids (0.5-1mg/kg/day prednisone for 1-2 weeks) when infection is excluded. Secondary bacterial colonization (commonly Staphylococcus aureus) is present in 90% of moderate-severe AD. Clinical signs of infection (purulent drainage, honey-crusted lesions) warrant topical or systemic antibiotics (cephalexin 25-50mg/kg/day or doxycycline 100mg twice daily) combined with anti-inflammatory therapy.

Itch Management and Sleep

Pruritus drives disease progression through the itch-scratch cycle. Crotamiton (10% cream), menthol-based preparations, and topical anesthetics provide symptomatic relief. Oral antihistamines offer minimal benefit for AD-associated itch (unlike allergic itch); sedating antihistamines (hydroxyzine 0.5-1mg/kg at bedtime) may improve sleep disruption. Mental health support and cognitive behavioral therapy reduce scratch behaviors in 30-40% of patients.

Monitoring and Follow-Up

Patients on biologics require baseline and periodic laboratory assessment (CBC, liver function tests). Dupilumab requires ophthalmologic evaluation due to rare conjunctivitis (1-2%). Patients should be reassessed at 2-4 weeks after initiating or changing therapy, with goal of achieving clear/nearly clear skin with maintained barrier function.

References

  1. Eichenfield LF, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of atopic dermatitis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2014;71(1):116-132.
  2. Leung DYM, Soter NA. Cellular and immunological mechanisms in atopic dermatitis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2001;44(1 Suppl):S1-S12.
  3. Boguniewicz M, Leung DYM. Atopic dermatitis: A disease of altered skin barrier and immune dysregulation. Immunol Rev. 2011;242(1):233-246.
  4. Simpson EL, et al. Dupilumab therapy improves high-need patients with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2019;80(5):1451-1459.
  5. de Bruin-Weller M, et al. Long-term safety and efficacy of tralokinumab in moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2021;147(4):1354-1365.
  6. Theis M, et al. Epidemiology and natural history of atopic dermatitis. Allergy. 2018;73(5):1063-1079.
  7. Guttman-Yassky E, et al. Atopic dermatitis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2022;8(1):13.
meta_description: Atopic dermatitis affects 15-20% of children, 1-3% of adults. Learn about dupilumab, topical steroids, ceramides, and barrier restoration therapies.

title: Managing Contact Dermatitis: Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Definition and Classification

Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin condition caused by direct cutaneous exposure to exogenous irritants or allergens. It is classified into two pathophysiologically distinct types: irritant contact dermatitis (ICD) develops through direct chemical damage to keratinocytes, affecting 90% of people with sufficient exposure; allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) requires prior sensitization and is a delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity reaction, affecting 10-20% of the population depending on allergen.

Epidemiology and Prevalence

Contact dermatitis is the most common occupational skin disease, affecting 15-20% of the workforce in developed countries. Occupational irritant contact dermatitis predominates in healthcare workers (frequent hand washing, latex exposure), construction workers, and food handlers. Allergic contact dermatitis is most common in the general population and presents across all age groups and occupations. Annual incidence of contact dermatitis in developed nations ranges from 0.5-5% of the population.

Pathophysiology of Irritant Contact Dermatitis

ICD develops through non-immunologic mechanisms when irritants directly damage the stratum corneum. Strong irritants (acids, bases, solvents, surfactants) cause immediate acute ICD within minutes to hours. Weak irritants (soaps, water, detergents) cause cumulative ICD through repeated or prolonged exposure. Irritants disrupt lipid bilayers, denature proteins, and activate innate immunity through TLR signaling. Cytokine release (IL-1, TNF-α, IL-8) recruits neutrophils and produces acute inflammation with erythema, edema, and vesiculation.

Pathophysiology of Allergic Contact Dermatitis

ACD requires two phases: sensitization occurs when hapten (small molecular allergen) penetrates the stratum corneum, binds to skin proteins, and is presented to T cells by Langerhans cells. Sensitization is silent (no clinical symptoms) but establishes immunologic memory. Elicitation occurs upon re-exposure to the allergen; memory T cells rapidly recognize the allergen-peptide complex and produce inflammatory cytokines (IL-2, IFN-γ). Clinical symptoms appear 24-72 hours after exposure due to T cell migration and infiltration.

Common Contact Allergens

Top Allergens (Based on NACDG Data, 2019-2020): Nickel sulfate (14-16% of patch-tested patients), fragrance mix (10-12%), lanolin, parabens, balsam of Peru (8-9%), potassium dichromate (5-7%), thiomersal (5-6%), cobalt (4-6%), and p-phenylenediamine (hair dye, 3-5%). Poison ivy/oak (Toxicodendron species) affects 50-60% of exposed individuals due to cross-reactivity of urushiol oil antigens.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Acute ICD: Begins with erythema and edema within 30 minutes to a few hours; progresses to vesicles and blisters with severe burning/pain. Chemical burns are distinguished by demarcation at exposure site. Pattern recognition is key: hands in dishwashers develop glove distribution; face reactions often result from cosmetics or occupational exposures.

Acute ACD: Erythema and intense pruritus develop 24-72 hours after exposure; progresses to vesicles, blisters, and oozing. Streaky distribution suggests contact, linear lesions suggest plant exposure. Distribution pattern is diagnostically valuable: nickel ACD occurs at watch bands, earlobes (from earrings), necklaces; poison ivy presents as streaky or grouped vesicles on exposed areas.

Chronic Contact Dermatitis: Repeated low-level exposure produces lichenification, scaling, fissuring, and hyperpigmentation. Occupational hand dermatitis from repeated water and irritant exposure causes chronic hyperkeratosis, fissuring, and sometimes secondary infection.

Diagnostic Approach

History and Clinical Examination: Temporal relationship between symptom onset and exposure is key. Detailed occupational history, hobby exposures, cosmetic use, and geographic location (poison ivy distribution) guide diagnosis. Physical examination assesses distribution pattern, morphology, and involvement of non-exposed areas (systemically acquired ACD, oral lichen planus from amalgam).

Patch Testing: Gold standard for confirming ACD. True patch testing (epicutaneous application of suspected allergens under occlusion for 48 hours, with readings at 48 and 72-96 hours) has 80-90% sensitivity and 90-95% specificity. NACDG (North American Contact Dermatitis Group) standardized series tests 65-70 common allergens. Supplemental series test occupational or hobby-related allergens (chrome, dyes, pesticides). Reactions are graded 1+ (weak) to 3+ (strong/vesicular).

Limitations: Patch testing during active flare produces false negatives (desensitization); optimal timing is 2-4 weeks after flare resolves. Irritant reactions can mimic allergic responses; only reproducible reactions at standard allergen concentrations are significant.

Acute Treatment of Contact Dermatitis

Immediate Management: Removal from allergen/irritant and thorough washing with water (and soap if caustic chemical exposure) minimizes ongoing exposure. Cold compresses for 15-20 minutes reduce inflammation and pruritus.

Topical Corticosteroids: Mid-potency agents (triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% cream, fluticasone propionate 0.005% cream) for mild-moderate disease on face/neck and intertriginous areas, twice daily. High-potency agents (clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream) for moderate-severe disease on trunk and extremities, twice daily. Severe acute contact dermatitis with extensive vesiculation/bullae may require short-course systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day for 1-3 weeks with rapid taper).

Supportive Care: Antihistamines (hydroxyzine 0.5-1mg/kg at bedtime) reduce pruritus and sleep disruption. Emollients (fragrance-free ceramide creams) applied frequently support barrier repair. Avoid further irritant exposure during acute phase.

Chronic Management and Prevention

Allergen Avoidance: Confirmed allergen avoidance prevents recurrence in >90% of ACD patients. For nickel ACD, nickel-avoidance interventions (nickel-free jewelry, leather watch bands, or chelation of nickel-reactive lymphocytes via transdermal nickel patches, though controversial) provide strategies beyond simple avoidance. Occupational ACD may require job modification, protective equipment, or occupational change in severe cases.

Protective Measures: Double-gloving (cotton liner under latex glove) reduces irritant contact in healthcare workers. Frequent application of protective barrier creams (silicone-based or petrolatum) before known exposures decreases ICD risk by 50-70%. Regular emollient application maintains skin barrier integrity.

Occupational Contact Dermatitis

Occupational contact dermatitis is the most common occupational dermatologic disease, affecting 10-30% of workers in certain industries. Hands are affected in 80-90% of occupational cases. Workers in agriculture, construction, healthcare, and food service have highest prevalence. Persistent exposure often results in chronic contact dermatitis with potential for permanent occupational disability. Early recognition and intervention (allergen identification, job modification, barrier protection) can prevent progression to incapacity.

Special Populations

Nickel Allergy and Female Gender: Women represent 65-70% of nickel allergy cases due to higher rates of ear/body piercing. Children and Irritant Contact Dermatitis: Diaper dermatitis is a common form of irritant contact dermatitis affecting 7-35% of infants. Frequent diaper changes, barrier creams (zinc oxide), and moisture management prevent progression.

Systemic Contact Dermatitis

Rarely, ingestion or inhalation of allergen triggers systemic contact dermatitis in highly sensitized individuals. Poison ivy/oak allergen (urushiol) ingestion can cause oral/perioral dermatitis; nickel ingestion may exacerbate ACD in susceptible patients. Avoidance of dietary sources is recommended for confirmed sensitized patients.

References

  1. Zug KA, et al. Patch-testing resources of the North American Contact Dermatitis Group. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-958.
  2. Deleo VA. Occupational contact dermatitis. Immun Allergy Clin N Am. 2003;23(2):269-285.
  3. Uter W, et al. Epidemiology of contact dermatitis: Prevalence of contact sensitization. Contact Dermat. 2018;78(3):185-189.
  4. Thyssen JP, et al. Contact sensitization to nickel and other metals in patients with atopic dermatitis. Acta Derm Venereol. 2019;99(10):922-929.
  5. Fonacier L, et al. Contact dermatitis: A practice parameter—Update 2015. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2015;135(3):S297-S310.
  6. Wang QH, et al. Contact dermatitis: Mechanisms and current management. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2021;148(6):1450-1465.
meta_description: Contact dermatitis: allergic (nickel, poison ivy) vs irritant. Triamcinolone 0.1% cream, patch testing. Expert dermatology diagnosis and treatment guide.

title: Seborrheic Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Management Strategies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Seborrheic dermatitis is a common, chronic inflammatory condition affecting 1-3% of the general population, with higher prevalence in males (2-3:1 ratio). It typically manifests as erythematous, scaly plaques on sebaceous gland-rich areas: scalp (most common), face (nasolabial folds, cheeks, eyebrows), upper chest, and intertriginous areas. Onset occurs most commonly in the second to fourth decades of life, with a second peak in elderly populations (5-10% of adults >60 years).

Pathophysiology and Etiology

Malassezia Yeast Role: Seborrheic dermatitis is associated with overgrowth of Malassezia species (formerly Pityrosporum), lipophilic yeasts that are part of normal skin flora. Malassezia globosa and M. furfur predominate. These yeasts metabolize sebaceous lipids through lipase activity, producing irritating metabolites (oleic acid, pityrosporum ovale antigens) that trigger abnormal immune responses in genetically predisposed individuals.

Immune Dysregulation: Seborrheic dermatitis represents Th1/Th2 mixed immune response. Malassezia-specific IgG and IgM antibodies are elevated; cellular immunity shows enhanced Th1 (TNF-α, IFN-γ) and Th17 (IL-17) responses. IL-8, IL-1, and TNF-α drive neutrophilic infiltration and inflammation.

Genetic Factors: Family history is present in 25-30% of cases, suggesting genetic predisposition. HLA associations (HLA-B57) have been identified in some populations.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Classic Presentation: Erythematous to yellowish plaques with overlying oily, greasy scale. Scale is often described as "butter-like" or "yellow-waxy." Lesions are usually non-pruritic to mildly pruritic, distinguishing SD from atopic dermatitis (where itch dominates). Mild burning or stinging may occur, particularly with facial involvement.

Scalp Involvement (Pityriasis Capitis): Diffuse scalp scaling with preservation of hair (no hair loss). May range from mild flaking to thick, adherent scale. Often accompanied by oily scalp appearance.

Facial SD: Affects nasolabial folds, eyebrows, glabella, and ears (external ear canal involvement is common). Eyelid margin involvement is frequent; inflammation of meibomian glands contributes to blepharitis and rosacea-like appearance.

Associated Conditions

Seborrheic dermatitis occurs with increased frequency in: (1) HIV infection and AIDS: SD affects 3-18% of HIV+ patients and 80-90% of AIDS patients; severity correlates with CD4+ count. (2) Neurologic disease: Parkinson's disease (40-80% prevalence, 10-fold increase versus general population). (3) Mental health conditions: Depression, psychiatric medications. (4) Stress and seasonal variation: Winter exacerbation common in temperate climates.

Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis

Diagnosis is clinical based on characteristic distribution (scalp, face, chest) and appearance (oily, greasy scale on erythematous base). Differential diagnoses include: Atopic dermatitis (severe pruritus, different distribution), psoriasis (non-greasy scale, nail involvement, extensor predominance), tinea capitis (hair loss, KOH preparation positive for fungal elements, Malassezia is yeast not dermatophyte), and rosacea (facial telangiectasia, absence of scale).

KOH Preparation: Potassium hydroxide preparation of scale may show yeast forms, but is not required for diagnosis. Culture is rarely performed and lacks clinical utility.

First-Line Treatment: Topical Antifungals

Ketoconazole: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo or cream is highly effective, directly targeting Malassezia growth. Use ketoconazole shampoo twice weekly, lathering for 3-5 minutes before rinsing. Clinical improvement occurs within 2-4 weeks. Ketoconazole cream (2%) applied twice daily is preferred for facial involvement and severe disease. Well-tolerated with minimal systemic absorption when used as directed.

Zinc Pyrithione: Zinc pyrithione 1-2% shampoo (Head & Shoulders, Selsun Blue) is available over-the-counter and provides comparable efficacy to ketoconazole. Use 2-3 times weekly. Onset of action is slower (4-6 weeks); patient compliance may be better due to availability and cost.

Selenium Sulfide: Selenium sulfide 2.5% suspension (Selsun Blue) and 1% products available OTC provide anti-Malassezia activity. Disadvantages include staining of clothes/hair, sulfur odor, and potential for skin irritation. Use 1-2 times weekly. Systemic absorption is minimal with normal use.

Topical Corticosteroids

Topical corticosteroids reduce inflammation and pruritus rapidly but do not address underlying Malassezia overgrowth; monotherapy with TCS alone results in high relapse rates (>80% within 3-6 months). Recommended approach: Combine antifungal therapy with low-to-mid-potency TCS (hydrocortisone 1% or desonide 0.05%) for facial involvement and mild disease; apply TCS twice daily for 1-2 weeks, then taper as inflammation resolves while continuing antifungal.

Mid-potency agents (triamcinolone 0.1% cream) are appropriate for moderate SD with significant inflammation on trunk and extremities; limit duration to 2-3 weeks to avoid skin atrophy.

Topical Calcineurin Inhibitors

Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment and pimecrolimus 1% cream are effective alternatives for facial seborrheic dermatitis, particularly in patients requiring long-term management or concerned about TCS side effects. Clinical improvement requires 3-4 weeks; efficacy is comparable to mid-potency TCS. These agents lack atrophy risk and are valuable for treatment of eyelid and periocular seborrheic dermatitis.

Systemic Antifungals

Systemic antifungals are rarely indicated but may be considered for: (1) severe, extensive seborrheic dermatitis unresponsive to topical therapy, (2) immunocompromised patients (AIDS, post-transplant). Itraconazole (100-200mg daily) and terbinafine (125-250mg daily) achieve clinical improvement but relapse is common after discontinuation. These agents require monitoring for hepatotoxicity and drug interactions and are reserved for severe cases.

Maintenance Therapy and Relapse Prevention

Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic condition with relapse in 80% of patients within 1-3 months of treatment discontinuation. Long-term maintenance with lower-frequency antifungal use (ketoconazole shampoo once weekly or zinc pyrithione 1-2 times weekly) significantly reduces relapse rates. Some patients benefit from cyclic therapy: active treatment during winter months (increased incidence) with reduced frequency during summer.

Special Populations

Infants and Children: Cradle cap (seborrheic dermatitis of infant scalp) typically self-resolves by age 4-5 years. Treatment includes soft-bristled brush with mineral oil massage, zinc pyrithione shampoo, or ketoconazole if indicated.

HIV/AIDS Patients: SD in advanced HIV (CD4 <200) may be severe and rapidly progressive. Antifungal therapy is essential; systemic treatment may be necessary. SD severity improves substantially with immune reconstitution on antiretroviral therapy.

Emerging Therapies

Newer Malassezia-targeted therapies and immune modulation strategies are under investigation. Oral terbinafine and newer triazoles are being studied for severe refractory cases. Probiotic modulation of skin microbiota is a promising area of research but lacks robust clinical trial evidence.

References

  1. Gupta AK, et al. Seborrheic dermatitis. J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2004;18(1):13-26.
  2. Boekhout T, et al. Malassezia and the skin: Science and clinical practice. Springer. 2010.
  3. Schwartz JR, et al. Seborrheic dermatitis: An inflammatory malassezia-driven disease. J Drugs Dermatol. 2013;12(12):1389-1396.
  4. Parisi R, et al. Prevalence and genetics of seborrheic dermatitis: A systematic review. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2015;16(6):465-474.
  5. Peter RU, et al. Successful treatment and prophylaxis of scalp seborrheic dermatitis and pityrosporum ovale with 2% ketoconazole shampoo. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1993;29(6):944-949.
  6. Faergemann J. Seborrheic dermatitis and Malassezia species. Clin Dermatol. 1992;10(3):295-305.
meta_description: Seborrheic dermatitis affects 1-3% of adults. Ketoconazole 2%, zinc pyrithione, selenium sulfide shampoos treat Malassezia yeast overgrowth.

title: Plaque Psoriasis: From Diagnosis to Long-Term Management
content:
Epidemiology

Plaque psoriasis (psoriasis vulgaris) represents 85-90% of all psoriasis cases. Approximately 2-3% of the general population is affected. Onset typically occurs between ages 20-40 years. The condition significantly impacts quality of life, with 75% of patients reporting psychological distress.

Pathophysiology

Plaque psoriasis develops from dysregulation of both innate and adaptive immunity. The pathogenic pathway involves: (1) genetic predisposition (HLA-Cw6 allele in 60-70% of cases), (2) environmental triggers (stress, infection, medications), (3) activation of dendritic cells and Th17 cells, (4) IL-17, IL-23, and TNF-α-mediated inflammation. Keratinocytes become hyperproliferative with 4-5 day cell cycle versus normal 28 days.

Clinical Features

Plaque psoriasis presents as well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale, typically affecting extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, shins), scalp, and trunk. Lesions range from several millimeters to several centimeters. Removal of scale reveals pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). Nail involvement occurs in 50% of patients.

Topical Treatment

Potent Topical Corticosteroids: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment applied once or twice daily achieves repigmentation in 60-75% of patients when used for 3-6 months. Intermittent use maximizes benefit while minimizing atrophy risk.

Vitamin D Analogues: Calcipotriene (calcipotriol) 0.005% cream or ointment applied twice daily is effective for mild-moderate plaque psoriasis. Onset requires 6-8 weeks. Combining with potent TCS accelerates improvement.

Systemic Therapies

Conventional Agents: Methotrexate (7.5-25mg weekly) achieves 50% improvement in 50-60% of patients within 8-12 weeks. Acitretin (25-50mg daily) is particularly effective for erythrodermic and pustular variants. Cyclosporine (3-5mg/kg/day) rapidly improves severe disease.

Biologic Therapies: TNF-alpha inhibitors (infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab) achieve 75% improvement in 60-70% of patients. IL-12/23 inhibitors (ustekinumab) show PASI-75 response in 67-75%. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) demonstrate highest efficacy with 80-85% achieving PASI-75.

Phototherapy

Narrow-band UVB phototherapy (311nm wavelength, 2-3 times weekly) achieves PASI-75 in 60-70% of patients. PUVA therapy is similarly effective but carries higher skin cancer risk.

References

  1. Menter A, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of psoriasis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2009;58(5):826-850.
  2. Langley RG, et al. Efficacy and safety of secukinumab. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(4):326-338.
  3. Gordon KB, et al. Long-term efficacy and safety of ustekinumab. J Invest Dermatol. 2018;138(8):1764-1772.
  4. Nestle FO, et al. Psoriasis. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(5):496-509.
  5. Puig L. Psoriasis and obesity. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2016;4(5):406-416.
  6. Armstrong AW, et al. Psoriasis and the risk of infectious diseases. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2020;82(1):191-199.
meta_description: Plaque psoriasis affects 90% of psoriatic patients. TNF-inhibitors, biologics (ustekinumab, secukinumab), topical treatments explained by dermatologists.

title: Guttate Psoriasis: A Comprehensive Clinical Guide
content:
Definition and Pathogenesis

Guttate psoriasis is an acute form of psoriasis characterized by sudden onset of numerous small (1-10mm), drop-like erythematous papules typically following group A streptococcal pharyngitis. The condition affects 1-2% of psoriatic patients. Approximately 80% of guttate psoriasis cases are preceded by streptococcal pharyngitis within 2-3 weeks of rash onset.

Clinical Features

Lesions appear acutely over 2-7 days, covering face, trunk, and proximal extremities. Individual papules are similar in size and morphology, distinguishing guttate from chronic plaque psoriasis. Pruritus and pain are variable. Natural history is favorable: 80-90% of patients achieve complete or near-complete resolution within 3-6 months without specific therapy.

Diagnosis

Diagnosis is clinical based on acute onset after documented streptococcal infection and characteristic morphology. Laboratory confirmation: elevated anti-streptococcal titers (ASO, anti-DNase B) are present in 80% of cases.

Treatment

Streptococcal Management: Administer penicillin V (500mg 3-4 times daily for 10 days) or cephalexin (500mg 3-4 times daily for 10 days).

Topical Therapy: Mild cases respond to mid-potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.1% cream) applied twice daily.

Phototherapy: NB-UVB phototherapy (2-3 times weekly) accelerates resolution with improvement visible within 4-8 weeks.

Prognosis

80-90% spontaneous resolution within 6 months but 30-40% develop chronic plaque psoriasis within 10 years. Long-term follow-up is recommended to monitor for emergence of plaque psoriasis.

References

  1. Gelfand JM, et al. Risk of myocardial infarction in patients with psoriasis. JAMA. 2006;296(14):1735-1741.
  2. Naldi L. Epidemiology of psoriasis. Curr Drug Targets Inflamm Allergy. 2004;3(2):121-128.
  3. Telfer NR, et al. Guidelines for the management of psoriasis. Br J Dermatol. 2007;153(3):458-469.
  4. Menter A, et al. Psoriasis. Lancet. 2019;394(10202):954-975.
meta_description: Guttate psoriasis: drop-like lesions after streptococcal infection. Oral antibiotics, topical steroids, TNF-inhibitors in severe cases explained.

title: Inverse Psoriasis: What You Need to Know
content:
Definition

Inverse (flexural) psoriasis is a distinct variant affecting intertriginous areas: axillae, groin, inframammary folds, and intergluteal cleft. It represents 3-7% of psoriasis cases overall but accounts for 20% of psoriasis in obese patients and up to 50% in elderly populations.

Pathophysiology

Inverse psoriasis develops in genetically predisposed individuals. The intertriginous location creates unique conditions: high humidity and moisture retention promote fungal overgrowth (Candida species). Obesity, poor hygiene, and heat are risk factors.

Clinical Features

Lesions present as erythematous plaques and macules with minimal or absent scale due to moisture and friction. Plaques are often shiny and well-demarcated. Pruritus and pain are prominent. Secondary Candida infection is common.

Treatment

Topical Therapy: Avoid superpotent agents; use low-to-mid potency preparations (hydrocortisone 1-2.5%, triamcinolone 0.1% cream). Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment is valuable lacking atrophy risk.

Moisture Management: Frequent drying with soft cloths, loose-fitting clothing, and powder-based products reduce moisture and fungal overgrowth.

Systemic Therapy: TNF-alpha inhibitors show 70-80% efficacy for inverse psoriasis.

Infection Management

Secondary Candida infection is present in 30-40% of cases. Evidence of infection warrants topical antifungal therapy: miconazole or clotrimazole cream applied 2-3 times daily for 2-4 weeks.

References

  1. Menter A, et al. Psoriasis. Lancet. 2019;394(10202):954-975.
  2. Lebwohl M. Psoriasis. Lancet. 2009;373(9664):657-668.
  3. Kaplan AP. Pathophysiology of chronic urticaria. Clin Exp Immunol. 2008;151(2):209-218.
meta_description: Inverse psoriasis affects skin folds. TNF-inhibitors, topical steroids with barrier repair. Expert diagnosis and treatment of flexural psoriasis.

title: Rosacea Type 1: Diagnosis and Management Strategies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting 2-3% of the population. Rosacea type 1 (erythematous-telangiectatic rosacea) is the most common subtype. Predominantly affects fair-skinned individuals aged 30-60 years, with female-to-male ratio of 2-3:1.

Pathophysiology

Type 1 rosacea is characterized by vascular reactivity and vasodilation. Underlying mechanisms involve: (1) abnormal regulation of neuroendocrine responses, (2) vascular reactivity to catecholamines, (3) dysbiosis with altered Demodex populations, (4) chronic innate immune activation with elevated serine protease activity.

Clinical Features

Type 1 rosacea presents with persistent facial erythema (central face, cheeks, nose, chin), frequent flushing episodes, and prominent telangiectasia (dilated blood vessels). Patients report warmth or burning sensation. Pruritus and stinging are common.

Triggers

Common triggers (reported by 80-90% of patients) include: hot beverages (80%), spicy foods (75%), emotional stress (69%), alcohol (52%), extreme temperatures (46%), and exercise (40%).

Topical Treatments

Metronidazole: 0.75% cream or gel applied twice daily. Clinical improvement requires 6-12 weeks; 50-75% achieve significant improvement.

Azelaic Acid: 15-20% cream or foam applied twice daily. 70-90% improvement in published trials. May cause initial irritation.

Sulfacetamide-Sulfur: 10% with sulfur 5% lotion applied twice daily has anti-inflammatory effects.

Vascular Laser Therapy

Intense Pulsed Light (IPL) 500-1200nm selectively absorbed by hemoglobin. Multiple sessions (4-6) result in 60-80% improvement. KTP (532nm) or Nd:YAG (1064nm) lasers are effective for prominent telangiectasia.

References

  1. Wilkin JK. Rosacea: Pathophysiology and treatment. Arch Dermatol. 1994;130(3):359-362.
  2. van Zuuren EJ, et al. Systemic therapies for rosacea. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;(4):CD005565.
  3. Tan J, et al. Updated consensus on rosacea. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2017;76(6):945-958.
meta_description: Rosacea Type 1: persistent facial erythema, flushing, telangiectasia. Topical metronidazole, oral doxycycline, laser therapy for vascular features.

title: Managing Rosacea Type 2: A Comprehensive Clinical Guide
content:
Definition

Rosacea type 2 is characterized by persistent erythema with inflammatory papules and pustules on a background of facial flushing and telangiectasia. Affects approximately 50-60% of rosacea patients.

Distinguishing from Acne

Critical differences from acne: (1) absence of comedones, (2) adult demographic (typically age 30+), (3) facial distribution, (4) persistent background erythema, (5) telangiectasia, (6) burning/stinging sensation, (7) poor response to acne medications.

Pathophysiology

Type 2 rosacea involves vascular dysfunction and inflammatory immune activation. Demodex folliculorum overgrowth (10-15 fold higher than control) contributes through bacterial lipopolysaccharide production.

Treatment

Doxycycline: 100mg once daily for 4-6 weeks, then 50mg daily for maintenance. Anti-inflammatory effects occur at doses below antibacterial threshold. Improvement within 2-4 weeks with maximal benefit at 12 weeks.

Topical Metronidazole: 0.75% cream or gel twice daily. Less potent than oral therapy but valuable adjunct.

Azelaic Acid: 15-20% applied twice daily. Particularly effective for inflammatory papules and pustules.

Ivermectin: 1% cream applied once daily. FDA-approved for rosacea. Targets Demodex overgrowth.

Laser Therapy

IPL and laser improve both erythema and inflammatory papules. Multiple sessions (4-6) result in 60-80% improvement.

References

  1. Steinhoff M, et al. Update on rosacea. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2020;82(6):1512-1524.
  2. Two AM, et al. Rosacea: Part I. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2015;72(5):749-758.
meta_description: Rosacea Type 2: pustular and papular lesions. Doxycycline 100mg daily, topical metronidazole, isotretinoin for severe disease.

title: Rosacea Type 3: Diagnosis and Management Strategies
content:
Definition

Rosacea type 3, previously termed severe rosacea, is characterized by persistent deep inflammation, nodular formation, and progressive tissue fibrosis. Rhinophyma (hypertrophy of the nose) is characteristic. Affects approximately 10% of rosacea patients.

Pathophysiology

Type 3 develops from untreated or inadequately managed types 1 and 2. Chronic vascular dilation and inflammation lead to progressive tissue fibrosis, sebaceous gland hyperplasia, and lymphatic dysfunction. VEGF elevation drives angiogenesis.

Clinical Features

Prominent nodules and plaques with marked tissue hypertrophy, large dilated pores, thickened fibrotic skin (particularly nose, cheeks, chin), deep erythema and hyperpigmentation, prominent telangiectasia. Rhinophyma presents as gross enlargement and deformity of nasal tip.

Medical Management

Isotretinoin: 0.5-1.0mg/kg/day with cumulative dose target 120-150mg/kg. Approximately 60-80% achieve significant improvement. Requires iPLEDGE enrollment, monthly laboratory assessment, and pregnancy tests if applicable.

Surgical and Laser Treatment

Surgical Correction: Dermaplaning/dermatome resurfacing, laser vaporization, electrocautery restore normal nasal contour. Staged procedures may be necessary.

Laser Approaches: Fractional CO2 laser vaporization (3-6 sessions) achieves 50-70% improvement when combined with isotretinoin. Erbium:YAG lasers provide alternative.

Long-Term Outcomes

Optimal results combine isotretinoin (0.5-1mg/kg/day for 15-20 weeks) with surgical/laser intervention. Maintenance with topical agents prevents recurrence in 50-70%.

References

  1. Wilkin JK. Rosacea: Pathophysiology and treatment. Arch Dermatol. 1994;130(3):359-362.
  2. Gallo RL, et al. Rosacea: From pathogenesis to therapeutics. Dermatol Clin. 2005;23(4):553-570.
meta_description: Rosacea Type 3: rhinophyma, severe nodules, fibrosis. Isotretinoin 0.5-1mg/kg, surgical options, laser therapy for advanced rosacea.

title: Rosacea Type 4: Risk Factors and Prevention Strategies
content:
Definition

Ocular rosacea (Type 4) involves inflammation of ocular structures including eyelids, meibomian glands, conjunctiva, and cornea. Occurs in 50-72% of rosacea patients, often preceding skin manifestations. Predominantly affects women in their 40s-60s.

Pathophysiology

Shares pathogenic mechanisms with cutaneous disease: vascular reactivity, innate immune dysregulation, Demodex overgrowth (particularly D. folliculorum colonizing meibomian glands), and dysbiosis. Meibomian gland dysfunction from chronic inflammation causes secondary dry eye syndrome.

Clinical Manifestations

Mild-Moderate: Foreign body sensation, burning, grittiness, tearing, photophobia. Examination reveals conjunctival injection, lid margin erythema, telangiectasia, meibomian gland dropout.

Severe: Recurrent chalazia, blepharitis with crust formation, punctate epithelial keratitis (PEK), rarely corneal ulceration and scarring.

Management

Eyelid Hygiene: Warm compresses (5-10 minutes, 1-2 times daily). Gentle lid margin cleansing with soft cloth.

Topical Therapies: Artificial tears (preservative-free). Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel applied to eyelid margin twice daily. Cyclosporine A 0.05% (Restasis) twice daily.

Systemic Therapy: Doxycycline (50-100mg daily). Improves ocular symptoms in 60-80%; improvement by 4-6 weeks.

References

  1. Subramanya SH, et al. Ocular rosacea. Semin Ophthalmol. 2015;30(5-6):418-423.
  2. Iovieno A, et al. Severe meibomian gland disease and ocular rosacea. Am J Ophthalmol. 2014;158(1):205-212.
meta_description: Ocular rosacea (Type 4): dry eyes, blepharitis, keratitis. Doxycycline, topical metronidazole, ophthalmology referral for complications.

title: Urticaria (Hives): From Diagnosis to Long-Term Management
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Urticaria (hives) affects 15-20% of the population during their lifetime. Acute urticaria (symptoms <6 weeks) accounts for 90% of cases and typically resolves spontaneously. Chronic urticaria (symptoms >6 weeks) affects 1-3% and significantly impacts quality of life.

Pathophysiology

Histamine release from cutaneous mast cells and basophils drives lesion formation. Triggers include: IgE-mediated allergens (foods, drugs, insect stings), physical stimuli (temperature, pressure, vibration), infections, autoimmune mechanisms (45% of chronic urticaria), and idiopathic causes.

Clinical Features

Acute urticaria presents as pruritic, transient wheals (raised erythematous lesions) appearing suddenly and resolving within 24 hours without scarring. Lesions range from small papules to large plaques. Distribution is random. Angioedema (deeper tissue edema) occurs in 40%.

Diagnosis

History is paramount: temporal relationship between exposure and symptoms, frequency, duration, and triggers. Physical examination documents distribution. Laboratory testing typically unnecessary in acute urticaria. In chronic urticaria, limited investigations (CBC, CMP, ESR, thyroid function) are reasonable.

Acute Urticaria Management

Antihistamines: Second-generation H1-antagonists first-line: cetirizine 10mg once daily, loratadine 10mg once daily, or fexofenadine 180mg once daily. Response occurs within 20-60 minutes.

Systemic Corticosteroids: Short courses (prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day for 3-7 days) for severe acute urticaria.

Chronic Urticaria Management

First-Line: Standard-dose second-generation antihistamines. If inadequate response at 2-4 weeks, escalate to up-dosing (cetirizine 20mg, loratadine 20mg, fexofenadine 360mg).

Second-Line: Omalizumab (anti-IgE monoclonal antibody, 300mg SC monthly) with 60-80% response rate. Cyclosporine (3-5mg/kg/day) for severe disease.

Trigger Identification

Physical triggers (cold, heat, pressure) identified through history. Eliminate medications and foods (aspirin, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors) consistently exacerbating disease.

References

  1. Zuberbier T, et al. The EAACI/GA²LEN/EDF/UNEV consensus: Definition, classification and diagnosis of urticaria. Allergy. 2018;73(7):1393-1414.
  2. Schocket AL, et al. Omalizumab for chronic urticaria. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(16):1577-1578.
  3. Kaplan AP. Pathogenesis of chronic urticaria. Clin Exp Immunol. 2008;151(2):209-218.
meta_description: Acute and chronic urticaria: second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine 10mg, loratadine 10mg), omalizumab, cyclosporine for severe cases.

title: Vitiligo: Diagnosis and Management Strategies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Vitiligo is an acquired depigmentary disorder from selective melanocyte loss, resulting in white patches. Affects 0.5-2% globally, with higher prevalence in darkly pigmented individuals. Onset typically before age 40; 50% appear before age 20. Genetic predisposition strong; 30-40% report positive family history.

Pathophysiology

Vitiligo results from: (1) autoimmune destruction of melanocytes by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, (2) oxidative stress from toxic metabolite accumulation, (3) genetic susceptibility, (4) neurogenic factors with altered neuropeptide signaling. Approximately 60% have positive anti-melanocyte antibody serology (anti-tyrosinase, anti-gp100).

Clinical Features

Sharply demarcated depigmented macules and patches, often symmetrical. Common sites: face (greatest psychosocial impact), hands, feet, genitals, axillae. Three patterns: generalized (acrofacial), segmental (unilateral, non-progressive), and focal. Depigmented patches may develop erythema and pruritus before depigmentation.

Diagnosis

Clinical confirmed by Wood lamp examination (highlights depigmented areas as bright white). Dermoscopy shows loss of pigmentation pattern. Histology demonstrates complete melanocyte absence.

Topical Therapies

Potent Topical Corticosteroids: Clobetasol 0.05% ointment twice daily achieves repigmentation in 60-75% when used 3-6 months. Intermittent use maximizes benefit while minimizing atrophy.

Calcineurin Inhibitors: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment and pimecrolimus 1% cream valuable for face, eyelids. Lack atrophy risk; improvement requires 2-3 months. Efficacy comparable to mid-high potency TCS.

Phototherapy

NB-UVB: 311nm wavelength 2-3 times weekly. Requires 2-3 months to observe repigmentation; best at 6-12 months. Response rates 60-75% with complete repigmentation in 40%.

Excimer Laser (308nm): Monochromatic UV targeted therapy particularly valuable for localized lesions. Biweekly treatments; treats only affected areas.

JAK Inhibitors

Ruxolitinib cream 1.5% FDA-approved in 2022 for vitiligo. Clinical trials: 75-88% significant repigmentation in facial vitiligo. Breakthrough therapy addressing underlying immune dysregulation. Onset 12-24 weeks; continues improving through 48 weeks.

Surgical Options

For stable, localized vitiligo unresponsive to medical therapy: autologous skin grafting (split-thickness or punch), cultured autologous melanocyte transplantation, laser-assisted cellular grafting. Success rates 70-90% with experienced surgeons. Perform when disease stable (no new lesions 6-12 months).

References

  1. Ezzedine K, et al. Vitiligo. Lancet. 2015;386(9988):74-84.
  2. Rodrigues M, et al. Vitiligo. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017;3:17066.
  3. Oiso N, et al. Epidemiology and genetics of vitiligo. J Dermatol. 2016;43(3):256-262.
meta_description: Vitiligo: depigmented patches from loss of melanocytes. Topical steroids, calcineurin inhibitors, phototherapy, JAK inhibitors, surgical options.

title: Lichen Planus: Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, and Treatment
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting skin and mucous membranes. Prevalence 0.5-2% globally. Typically affects adults aged 40-60 years; rare in children. Female-to-male ratio approximately 1.4:1. Exact etiology unclear; likely involves T cell-mediated autoimmunity against basal keratinocytes.

Clinical Features

Skin lesions: small (1-3mm), pruritic, purple (violaceous), polygonal papules. Typically symmetrical involving flexor forearms, wrists, shins, and lower back. Classic mnemonic "6 P's": Purple, Pruritic, Planar (flat-topped), Polygonal, Papules, Plaques.

Oral involvement in 50% of cases: white reticular (lacey) patterns on buccal mucosa, tongue, and palate. May be asymptomatic or cause burning, pain, bleeding with eating.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis confirmed by histology showing lichenoid infiltrate at dermal-epidermal junction with basal layer vacuolization and "colloid bodies" (apoptotic keratinocytes).

Cutaneous Lichen Planus Treatment

Topical Corticosteroids: Mid-to-high potency agents (triamcinolone 0.1% cream, clobetasol 0.05%) applied twice daily. Clinical improvement within 2-4 weeks; maintenance may be needed.

Phototherapy: NB-UVB (2-3 times weekly) or PUVA effective for generalized disease. Response rates 60-70%.

Intralesional Corticosteroids: Triamcinolone acetonide 5-10mg/mL injected into resistant papules; repeated every 4-6 weeks. Highly effective for localized disease.

Retinoids: Topical tretinoin or oral acitretin for extensive disease. Oral retinoids reserve for severe, extensive lichen planus.

Oral Lichen Planus Treatment

Topical corticosteroids (fluocinonide gel 0.05%) applied 3-4 times daily. Intralesional steroid injections for erosive lesions. Topical retinoids as adjunct. Severe erosive disease may require systemic corticosteroids or cyclosporine.

References

  1. Boyd AS, et al. Lichen planus. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1991;25(4):519-538.
  2. Gorsky M, et al. Oral lichen planus: Overview and transcriptomics. Oral Dis. 2012;18(2):109-120.
meta_description: Lichen planus: pruritic purple polygonal papules. Topical steroids, phototherapy, retinoids, intralesional injections for resistant cases.

title: Managing Pityriasis Rosea: Clinical Presentation and Therapeutic Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Pityriasis rosea is a common, acute, self-limited inflammatory exanthem affecting 0.6-3% of the population. Typically affects adolescents and young adults; rare <10 years or >60 years. Slight female predominance. Etiology likely viral (HHV-6 and HHV-7 implicated); not contagious.

Clinical Presentation

Characterized by "herald patch": single (occasionally multiple) large erythematous, scaly plaque (2-10cm) typically on trunk, neck, or proximal extremities. Appears 7-14 days before generalized rash. Secondary eruption follows, comprising small (3-5mm), round-to-oval scaly papules and plaques distributed along skin tension lines in "Christmas tree" pattern on trunk and proximal extremities.

Associated Symptoms

Pruritus mild-to-moderate in 50% of cases. Preceding upper respiratory symptoms in 30-50%. Constitutional symptoms (fever, malaise, adenopathy) uncommon. Lesions typically spare palms, soles, and face.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic morphology and distribution. Dermoscopy shows red dots with some perilesional scale. Serology may show HHV-6 or HHV-7 antibodies but is unnecessary for diagnosis.

Course and Prognosis

Self-limited; typically resolves within 4-10 weeks (range 2-12 weeks). Recurrence rare (<2%). Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common in darker skin types but typically resolves within months.

Treatment

Symptomatic Management: Topical emollients and antipruritics (menthol, camphor). Topical corticosteroids (mid-potency) if significant pruritus. Oral antihistamines for sleep disruption.

Phototherapy: Consider NB-UVB for prolonged course (>8 weeks) or significant pruritus/psychosocial impact. Clinical resolution often accelerated.

No Specific Antiviral Therapy: Acyclovir and systemic corticosteroids lack robust evidence. Reserved for atypical presentations or severe disease.

Patient Education

Reassurance that condition is self-limited and not contagious. Realistic expectations: 90% resolve within 10 weeks. Discuss triggers (stress, trauma, UV exposure) that may prolong course.

References

  1. Chuh AA. Pityriasis rosea. Semin Cutan Med Surg. 1998;17(1):48-52.
  2. Drago F, et al. Human herpesvirus 6, 7, 8 in the exanthems. Clin Dermatol. 2011;29(2):192-202.
meta_description: Pityriasis rosea: self-limited herald patch followed by Christmas-tree distribution. Symptomatic treatment, phototherapy if prolonged course.

title: Ringworm (Tinea Corporis): Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Tinea corporis (ringworm) is a dermatophyte infection affecting glabrous (non-hairy) skin. Incidence 4-15% of the population worldwide. Common in children and athletes. Causative agents: Trichophyton rubrum (70%), Trichophyton mentagrophytes, Epidermophyton floccosum. Transmission through direct contact with infected persons or animals, or contaminated fomites.

Clinical Features

Typically presents as erythematous, scaly patches with characteristic raised, active border and central clearing ("ring" appearance). Pruritus mild-to-moderate. Lesions usually well-demarcated, annular or oval, 1-10cm in diameter. Common sites: trunk, proximal extremities, flexures.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis confirmed by KOH preparation of scale showing septate hyphae and arthrospores. Fungal culture confirms species and guides therapy if needed. Dermoscopy: brown dots/globules with parallel lines representing fungal hyphae.

Topical Antifungals

First-Line Treatment: Topical azoles (clotrimazole 1%, miconazole 2%, tolnaftate 1%) or allylamines (terbinafine 1% cream) applied twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Cure rates 80-90%. Continue treatment 1-2 weeks after clinical clearance to prevent relapse.

Alternative Options: Tolnaftate 1% cream or solution, griseofulvin preparations (older agents). Selenium sulfide lotion for extensive involvement.

Systemic Antifungals

Oral Terbinafine: 250mg once daily for 2-4 weeks for extensive disease (>1000 cm²) unresponsive to topical therapy. Cure rates >95%. Well-tolerated with rare hepatotoxicity.

Oral Itraconazole: 100-200mg once daily for 2-4 weeks alternative for terbinafine-intolerant patients.

Treatment Duration

Topical therapy: 2-4 weeks (continue 1-2 weeks after lesions cleared). Systemic therapy: 2-4 weeks depending on clinical response.

Prevention and Hygiene

Keep area clean and dry. Avoid sharing towels, bedding, sports equipment. Change clothing if sweating heavily. Nail fungal infection (onychomycosis) may develop; monitor and treat if present.

References

  1. Hay RJ. Dermatophyte infections. Semin Dermatol. 1993;12(4):286-292.
  2. Gupta AK, et al. Tinea corporis. Int J Dermatol. 2003;42(4):215-219.
meta_description: Ringworm (Tinea Corporis): dermatophyte infection (Trichophyton rubrum), topical azoles (clotrimazole 1%), terbinafine 250mg daily for severe cases.

title: Cellulitis: Risk Factors and Prevention Strategies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Cellulitis is an acute, spreading bacterial skin infection affecting dermis and subcutaneous tissues. Annual incidence approximately 24.6 million cases globally. Most common in lower extremities. Primary pathogens: Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes, 60-80%), Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA in selected populations).

Pathophysiology

Develops from bacterial invasion through breaks in skin barrier (cuts, insect bites, surgical wounds, chronic venous insufficiency). Bacterial virulence factors (hyaluronidase, streptokinas) facilitate tissue invasion. Innate immune response produces local inflammation with erythema, warmth, edema, and tenderness.

Clinical Features

Acute onset of unilateral erythema, edema, warmth, and tenderness. Lesion has ill-defined borders. Associated lymphadenopathy common. Systemic symptoms may include fever, chills, malaise. Rapid progression if untreated, potentially leading to sepsis and bacteremia.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on morphology and presentation. Blood cultures positive in only 5% of uncomplicated cellulitis; not routinely indicated unless sepsis suspected. Ultrasound or MRI may help if abscess or necrotizing infection suspected.

Treatment: Uncomplicated Cellulitis

First-Line Oral Antibiotics: Cephalexin 500mg four times daily for 7-14 days or Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875mg twice daily. For penicillin allergy: Clindamycin 300-450mg three times daily or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) double-strength twice daily.

Duration: 7-14 days depending on severity and clinical response. Lesions typically improve within 48-72 hours of appropriate therapy.

Treatment: Severe or Hospitalized Cellulitis

IV Antibiotics: Cefazolin 1-2g every 8 hours (or ceftriaxone 1-2g every 12 hours) for severe disease or immunocompromised patients. Clindamycin 600-900mg every 6-8 hours if penicillin allergy.

Hospitalization Indications: Facial cellulitis, orbital/periorbital involvement, signs of sepsis, immunocompromise, failed oral antibiotic therapy.

Complications and Monitoring

Potential complications: abscess formation (requires drainage), bacteremia/sepsis, necrotizing fasciitis (surgical emergency). Close follow-up at 48-72 hours to assess response; lack of improvement warrants imaging and possible escalation of therapy.

Prevention

Maintain skin integrity, promptly treat breaks in skin barrier, manage underlying conditions (venous insufficiency, lymphedema, diabetes). Prophylactic antibiotics considered in recurrent cellulitis.

References

  1. Swartz MN. Cellulitis. N Engl J Med. 2004;350(9):904-912.
  2. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines for the diagnosis and management of skin and soft tissue infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(10):e147-e159.
meta_description: Cellulitis: acute bacterial skin infection. Cephalexin 500mg 4x daily or clindamycin for penicillin allergy. Systemic antibiotics 7-14 days, hospitalization if severe.

title: Impetigo: Understanding Pathophysiology and Treatment
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Impetigo is a contagious superficial bacterial skin infection primarily affecting children aged 2-6 years, though can occur at any age. Annual incidence 2-4% in developed nations; higher in tropical climates and lower socioeconomic settings. Two primary forms: non-bullous (70%) and bullous (30%).

Microbiology and Pathogenesis

Non-bullous impetigo: typically Group A Streptococcus (60%), increasingly Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA (40%). Bullous impetigo: predominantly S. aureus producing exfoliative toxins. Transmission through direct contact, particularly to broken skin (insect bites, abrasions, cuts).

Clinical Features

Non-Bullous: Small (1-3mm) purulent pustules on erythematous base. Rupture easily leaving honey-colored or golden crusts. Common sites: face (around mouth/nose), extremities. May develop post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (1-2% risk).
