Bullous: Large (0.5-3cm) fluid-filled blisters (bullae) on normal or minimally erythematous skin. Blisters rupture easily leaving thin, golden-yellow crusts and exposed erosions. More often caused by S. aureus.

Diagnosis and Differential

Clinical diagnosis based on morphology. Gram stain and culture confirm organism and guide therapy if treatment failure. Differential includes: Ecthyma (deeper infection with scarring), herpes simplex, varicella.

Treatment: Topical

Topical Mupirocin: Mupirocin 2% ointment applied 2-3 times daily for 5-7 days. Effective for localized non-bullous impetigo (few lesions, <2% BSA). Avoid mupirocin on large surface areas due to potential systemic absorption.

Efficacy: Cure rates >90% for localized disease. Systemic application rate negligible with normal use.

Treatment: Systemic Antibiotics

First-Line Oral: Cephalexin 500mg four times daily (25-50mg/kg/day for children) for 7-10 days. Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875mg twice daily alternative.

Penicillin Allergy: Clindamycin 300mg three times daily (15mg/kg/day for children, divided 3x daily) for 7-10 days.

MRSA Concerns: If MRSA suspected or confirmed, use clindamycin or TMP-SMX (double-strength twice daily for adults).

Special Considerations

Bullous Impetigo: Requires systemic antibiotics due to depth of infection and exfoliative toxin-mediated pathology. Oral cephalexin or clindamycin for 10-14 days.

Post-Streptococcal Complications: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) occurs in 1-2% of impetigo cases. Manage with fluid and electrolyte monitoring; renal function usually recovers without specific intervention.

Prevention and Infection Control

Cover lesions, avoid contact until 48 hours of antibiotic therapy. Exclude from daycare/school per guidelines. Handwashing and good hygiene prevent spread. Manage underlying skin conditions (eczema, insect bites).

References

  1. O'Neill AM, et al. Impetigo: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2018;98(10):625-633.
  2. Stevens DL, et al. Impetigo. Dermatol Clin. 2011;29(3):413-425.
meta_description: Impetigo: contagious superficial bacterial skin infection. Topical mupirocin 2% or oral cephalexin 500mg 4x daily for 7-10 days.

title: Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV): A Comprehensive Clinical Guide
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Herpes simplex virus (HSV) affects approximately 67% of the global population <50 years, with prevalence increasing with age. Two types: HSV-1 (primarily oral, 67% seroprevalence) and HSV-2 (primarily genital, 11% seroprevalence). Transmission through direct contact with infected oral or genital secretions. Establishes latency in dorsal root ganglia with periodic reactivation.

Pathophysiology and Viral Replication

Primary infection involves viral entry through mucosal epithelium, local replication, and retrograde transport along sensory nerves to ganglia. Vesicular lesions form from focal areas of epithelial necrosis and inflammatory infiltrate. Latency maintained by viral and host immune mechanisms; reactivation triggered by stress, fever, immunosuppression, UV exposure, or menstrual cycle.

Clinical Features: Primary Infection

Primary HSV typically presents with systemic symptoms (fever, malaise, adenopathy) followed by grouped vesicles on erythematous base. Vesicles rupture leaving painful erosions. Associated severe pain, dysuria (genital), or pharyngitis. Lesions crust and heal within 7-10 days. Viral shedding and infectivity peak during vesicular and early erosive stages.

Clinical Features: Recurrent Infection

Recurrences involve prodromal symptoms (tingling, burning) 1-2 days before visible lesions. Lesions fewer in number, more localized, and heal more rapidly (3-5 days) than primary infection. Frequency varies: some patients experience few episodes lifetime; others 4-6 recurrences annually. Post-herpetic neuralgia rare with HSV but common with zoster.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis confirmed by: (1) PCR of vesicular fluid (most sensitive), (2) HSV culture (takes 2-7 days; declining sensitivity), (3) Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells; insufficient for confirmation), (4) serology (IgG antibodies for past infection, IgM for primary infection).

Treatment: Primary Infection

First-Line: Acyclovir 400-800mg five times daily for 7-10 days initiated within 72 hours of symptom onset for maximal efficacy. For genital herpes: acyclovir 400mg five times daily × 10 days or valacyclovir 1g 3x daily × 10 days.

IV Therapy: Acyclovir 5-10mg/kg IV every 8 hours for severe disseminated disease, encephalitis, or immunocompromised patients.

Treatment: Recurrent Infection

Episodic Therapy: Acyclovir 400mg 3x daily × 5 days or valacyclovir 500mg twice daily × 3 days started at prodrome or within 24 hours of lesion onset. Modest reduction in lesion duration and pain.

Suppressive Therapy: Acyclovir 400mg twice daily or valacyclovir 500mg once daily for patients with ≥6 recurrences annually or significant psychosocial impact. Reduces recurrence frequency by 70-80%; continued indefinitely or reassessed annually.

Topical Therapy

Topical acyclovir or penciclovir creams applied 5-6 times daily for 4-5 days provide modest symptomatic benefit if initiated early. Most benefit seen with primary infection; minimal impact on recurrent disease. Systemic therapy always preferred.

Management of Complications

HSV Encephalitis: Medical emergency requiring IV acyclovir 10mg/kg every 8 hours. MRI/lumbar puncture for diagnosis. Mortality 10-20% untreated; decreases to 15% with treatment.

Neonatal HSV: Vertical transmission occurs during vaginal delivery. Infants develop systemic disease (sepsis, meningitis, encephalitis) requiring urgent IV acyclovir 10mg/kg every 8 hours.

Disseminated HSV: Rare but potentially life-threatening. IV acyclovir therapy essential; hospitalization required.

Long-Term Management

Counsel regarding transmission (infectivity during prodrome and vesicular stages; minimal during healing). Recommend barrier protection (condoms) and disclosure to partners. Suppressive therapy for patients with frequent recurrences, immunosuppression, or genital HSV with high transmission risk.

References

  1. Whitley RJ. Herpes simplex encephalitis: Adolescents and adults. Antiviral Res. 2006;71(2-3):141-148.
  2. Wald A. Herpes simplex virus type 2 transmission. Curr Opin Infect Dis. 2004;17(1):15-21.
  3. Solomon L, et al. Management of herpes simplex virus infection in pregnancy. Obstet Gynecol. 2016;127(1):1-25.
meta_description: Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV): acyclovir 400mg 5x daily, valacyclovir, suppressive therapy, primary and recurrent infection management.

title: Herpes Zoster (Shingles): Clinical Presentation and Therapeutic Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Herpes zoster (shingles) results from reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) in dorsal root ganglia following primary varicella infection or vaccination. Incidence 1.2-3.4 per 1000 person-years in immunocompetent hosts; significantly higher in immunocompromised and elderly (>60 years). Lifetime risk of zoster approximately 30%. Recurrence uncommon (3-4% of cases).

Pathophysiology

VZV remains latent in sensory ganglia after primary infection. Immunological decline, stress, radiation therapy, or malignancy allows viral reactivation. Virus travels along sensory nerve distributions causing dermatomal vesicular eruption. Intense inflammation and nerve involvement result in severe pain (often the most distressing symptom).

Clinical Features: Early Stage

Prodromal phase (2-7 days) characterized by dermatomal pain, burning, pruritus, or paresthesias without visible rash. Dysesthesia and hyperesthesia of affected dermatome. Constitutional symptoms (fever, malaise, headache) common. Thoracic (55%) and cervical (15%) dermatomes most frequently affected.

Clinical Features: Vesicular Stage

Grouped vesicles on erythematous base in dermatomal distribution. Vesicles umbilicate and crust within 7-10 days. Pustulization from secondary bacterial infection common. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation frequent. Unilateral involvement distinguishes zoster from disseminated varicella.

Complications

Post-Herpetic Neuralgia (PHN): Persistent pain (>3 months post-rash onset) in 10-50% of patients; increases with age (>50% risk in patients >60 years). Most bothersome complication causing significant quality-of-life impairment and disability.

Other Complications: Zoster ophthalmicus (V1 involvement) causing ocular complications (keratitis, uveitis, vision loss), Bell palsy (VII involvement), meningitis/encephalitis (rare).

Treatment: Antiviral Therapy

First-Line: Acyclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days initiated within 72 hours of rash onset. Reduces acute pain duration and post-herpetic neuralgia risk by ~50%.

Alternative Options: Valacyclovir 1g three times daily × 7 days (superior bioavailability; dosing convenience) or famciclovir 500mg three times daily × 7 days (similar to valacyclovir).

IV Therapy: Acyclovir 10-15mg/kg every 8 hours for disseminated zoster, ophthalmic zoster, meningitis, encephalitis, or immunocompromised patients.

Adjunctive Treatments for Pain

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisone 30mg daily × 7 days, then taper over 2 weeks reduces acute pain and improves function; minimal impact on PHN development. Benefit greatest if initiated within 72 hours.

Gabapentinoids: Gabapentin starting 100mg three times daily escalating to 1800-3600mg daily in divided doses or pregabalin 150mg daily escalating to 600mg daily. Most effective for PHN prevention if initiated early (during acute phase).

Topical Agents: Capsaicin cream 0.075% applied 3-5 times daily to healed lesions provides PHN relief. Lidocaine patches applied to painful areas. Avoid application to open lesions.

Management of Post-Herpetic Neuralgia

Preventive approach superior to treatment. Early antiviral therapy, adjunctive corticosteroids, and gabapentinoids initiated during acute phase reduce PHN incidence and severity. Established PHN treated with gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 25-75mg nightly), topical capsaicin or lidocaine patches, and potentially opioids for refractory cases.

Vaccination

Zoster vaccine (Shingrix, recombinant subunit vaccine) recommended for all adults ≥50 years and immunocompromised age ≥19 years. Two-dose series 2-6 months apart. Efficacy >90% in preventing zoster and >90% in preventing PHN. Two doses provide durable protection.

References

  1. Sampathkumar P, et al. Herpes zoster (shingles). Mayo Clin Proc. 2009;84(3):274-280.
  2. Dworkin RH, et al. Diagnosis and assessment of pain associated with herpes zoster. J Pain. 2008;9(1 Suppl):S37-S44.
  3. Gnann JW. Varicella-zoster virus: Atypical presentations and unusual complications. J Infect Dis. 2002;186(Suppl 1):S91-S98.
  4. Yawn BP, et al. Herpes zoster and postherpetic neuralgia: Prevention and management. Mayo Clin Proc. 2016;91(12):1645-1653.
meta_description: Herpes Zoster (Shingles): varicella-zoster virus reactivation. Acyclovir 800mg 5x daily, valacyclovir 1g 3x daily for 7 days. Post-herpetic neuralgia prevention crucial.

title: Managing Warts (Verruca Vulgaris): Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Warts are benign proliferative skin lesions caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. Prevalence 7-12% in general population; higher in children and young adults. Over 100 HPV types identified; common cutaneous types include HPV-1, HPV-2, HPV-4, HPV-27, HPV-29. Transmission through direct contact with infected individuals or contaminated surfaces; autoinoculation common.

Pathophysiology

HPV infects basal keratinocytes, triggering keratinocyte proliferation and hyperkeratinization. Viral presence stimulates cellular proliferation without cell transformation (benign lesions). Immune control determines resolution; most warts (90%) spontaneously resolve within 2 years, though may persist indefinitely.

Clinical Types

Common Warts: Firm, discrete, hyperkeratotic papules (2-8mm) typically on hands, fingers, elbows, knees. May have central "thrombosed vessels" (black dots).

Plantar Warts: Hyperkeratotic lesions on plantar surface often with central black dots. Painful due to pressure and location.

Palmoplantar Warts: Occur on palms and soles; mosaic pattern with multiple coalesced lesions.

Flat Warts: Flat-topped, small (1-3mm), numerous papules typically on face, hands, trunk; skin-colored or slightly hyperpigmented.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on morphology and distribution. Dermoscopy shows characteristic vascular patterns (punctate, comma-like, or hairpin vessels). HPV typing unnecessary for cutaneous warts; reserved for genital/plantar types if malignancy suspected.

Treatment Options: First-Line

Cryotherapy: Liquid nitrogen applied every 2-4 weeks until resolution (average 3-4 treatments). Efficacy 50-70%; modest pain. Cryotherapy induces local immune response facilitating clearance.

Salicylic Acid: 17% cream or solution applied daily after soaking in water for 10 minutes. Cover with occlusive dressing. Treatment duration weeks to months. Efficacy 50-60%; excellent for widespread lesions. Caustic agent; protect surrounding skin.

Imiquimod: 5% cream applied 3-5 times weekly for 12-16 weeks. Toll-like receptor agonist stimulating local immune response. Efficacy 50-60%; slower onset than cryotherapy but good for difficult-to-treat areas.

Treatment Options: Second-Line

Podofilox: 0.5% solution or gel applied twice daily for 3 days weekly. Cytotoxic agent; efficacy 50-60%. Contraindicated in pregnancy and large surface area application.

Bleomycin: Intralesional injection (0.1-0.5mg per lesion) repeated every 3-4 weeks. Highly effective (90%) for resistant warts. Systemic absorption risk with extensive treatment; monitor renal function.

Treatment in Special Populations

Plantar Warts: Address causative factors (pressure, moisture). Cryotherapy, salicylic acid, or combination therapy. Pain management essential. Orthotic modifications (padding) provide symptomatic relief.

Extensive or Refractory Warts: Consider systemic retinoids (oral isotretinoin) or photodynamic therapy for extensive involvement unresponsive to standard modalities.

Natural History and Counseling

Majority (90%) spontaneously resolve within 2 years. Treatment accelerates resolution but high recurrence rates (20-50%) common. Set realistic expectations; reassurance about benign nature reduces anxiety. Avoid self-treatment beyond topical agents due to infection/scarring risk.

Prevention

HPV vaccines (Gardasil 9) prevent multiple cutaneous HPV types. Recommended for ages 11-45 years. Although primarily marketed for genital cancer prevention, emerging evidence suggests benefit for cutaneous warts.

References

  1. Gibbs S, et al. Local treatments for cutaneous warts. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2012;(9):CD001781.
  2. Sterling JC. Viral infections. Rook's Textbook of Dermatology. 2016.
  3. Kwok CS, et al. Topical treatments for cutaneous warts. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2012;(9):CD001781.
meta_description: Warts (Verruca Vulgaris): human papillomavirus (HPV). Cryotherapy, salicylic acid 17%, imiquimod 5%, podofilox, or intralesional bleomycin for resistant cases.

title: Molluscum Contagiosum: Clinical Presentation and Therapeutic Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Molluscum contagiosum is a benign viral infection caused by molluscipoxvirus (genus Molluscipoxvirus). Affects 1-2% of dermatology clinic patients; higher in children and sexually active individuals. Transmission through direct contact or contaminated fomites (towels, clothing, toys). Most lesions spontaneously resolve within 6-12 months in immunocompetent patients; may persist indefinitely in immunosuppressed.

Pathophysiology

Virus replicates in keratinocytes, producing characteristic inclusion bodies (molluscum bodies) composed of viral particles. Lesions represent proliferative response with virus-laden cytoplasm. Immune response typically clears infection; persistent lesions suggest incomplete immune control.

Clinical Features

Dome-shaped, flesh-colored to pink papules (2-5mm, occasionally larger) with characteristic central umbilication containing waxy material. Lesions often numerous (5-20+), clustered or scattered. Mild pruritus or surrounding inflammation indicates host immune response approaching resolution. Common sites: face, neck, trunk, extremities; sexually transmitted form on genitals/inner thighs.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic morphology (dome-shaped, umbilicated papules). Histology shows characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies (molluscum bodies). Electron microscopy or PCR useful if diagnosis uncertain but unnecessary for routine cases.

Natural History

Self-limited in immunocompetent individuals; 90% resolve within 6-12 months. Spontaneous immune response produces localized inflammation around lesions ("molluscum dermatitis") heralding resolution. Lesions may cluster at site of irritation (autoinoculation through scratching, shaving, etc.). Secondary bacterial infection possible.

Treatment: Observation

Watchful waiting appropriate for asymptomatic lesions in immunocompetent patients given natural resolution. Explain to patient that lesions typically resolve within 6-12 months. However, many patients prefer active treatment for cosmetic reasons or to prevent spread, particularly in children and athletes.

Treatment: Topical Options

Imiquimod: 5% cream applied 3-5 times weekly under occlusion for 12-16 weeks. Activates toll-like receptors stimulating local immune response. Efficacy 60-90%; well-tolerated. Excellent for extensive disease.

Tretinoin: 0.025-0.1% cream applied nightly. Modest efficacy; useful as adjunctive agent. Causes irritation; initiate at low concentration.

Salicylic Acid: 17% solution applied daily to lesions. Modest efficacy; economical option.

Treatment: Destructive Options

Cryotherapy: Liquid nitrogen applied every 2-4 weeks until resolution. Efficacy 50-70%; modest pain and post-inflammatory changes. Average 3-4 treatments for resolution.

Curettage: Mechanical removal using sharp curette under local anesthesia or with topical anesthetic cream (eutectic mixture of lidocaine and prilocaine, EMLA). Highly effective single treatment; leaves small punctate scars. May be combined with topical cidofovir or potassium hydroxide.

Topical Potassium Hydroxide (KOH): Application of dilute KOH solution causing chemical destruction. Efficacy variable; risk of irritation and scarring limits widespread use.

Special Populations

Severe Immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS): Lesions may become numerous and persistent. HAART therapy often results in immune reconstitution and lesion clearance. Treatment options same but may require more aggressive intervention.

Atopic Individuals: Higher risk of spread and secondary infection; active treatment often recommended.

Prevention

Avoid sharing personal items (towels, clothing, razors). Cover lesions to prevent transmission. Avoid shaving or manipulation of lesions. Athletes should avoid skin-to-skin contact sports until lesions treated.

References

  1. Gottlieb SL, et al. Molluscum contagiosum. Am Fam Physician. 2005;72(12):2323-2328.
  2. Olson ME, et al. Molluscum contagiosum: Review of treatment options. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2013;68(1):S74-S79.
meta_description: Molluscum Contagiosum: poxvirus (MCV-1), self-limited in immunocompetent. Cryotherapy, curettage, imiquimod for symptomatic treatment.

title: Scabies: Understanding Pathophysiology and Treatment
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Scabies is a parasitic infestation caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis. Affects approximately 300 million people annually worldwide. Transmission through prolonged skin-to-skin contact (sexual contact, sharing bedding) or rarely through contaminated fomites. Incubation period 4-6 weeks for primary infestation; 1-3 days for reinfection (due to prior sensitization).

Pathophysiology and Clinical Features

Female mites burrow into stratum corneum creating tunnels where they lay eggs. Intense pruritus results from allergic hypersensitivity reaction to mite, feces, and secretions. Characteristic S-shaped burrows visible on skin surface, particularly on hands, fingers, wrists, genitals, buttocks. Lesions often excoriated from scratching; secondary bacterial infection common.

Clinical forms: (1) typical scabies with classic burrows and pruritus; (2) bullous scabies with blistering lesions; (3) Norwegian scabies (crusted scabies) in immunosuppressed patients with thick crusts harboring thousands of mites.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on distribution, pruritus (particularly nocturnal and bilateral involvement), and family/contact history. Confirmation: (1) dermoscopy showing characteristic "jet with contrail" sign (mite and burrow), (2) scabies preparation (mineral oil mount of scale; shows mites, eggs, or feces), (3) dermoscopy showing mite, eggs, or feces.

Treatment: Topical Therapy

Permethrin 5% Cream: Gold standard for typical scabies. Apply from neck to toes (including scalp for children <2 years) after bathing; leave on 8-14 hours (typically overnight), then wash off. Repeat application 7-14 days later to kill newly hatched mites. Efficacy >95%; safe even in infants >2 months.

Sulfur 5-10% Ointment: Alternative for infants and pregnant women. Apply nightly for 3 consecutive nights. Less effective than permethrin; preferred in special populations.

Benzyl Benzoate 10-25%: Less commonly used in US but effective. Apply nightly for 3 nights. Caustic; irritating to skin and mucous membranes.

Treatment: Systemic Therapy

Ivermectin: Oral dose 200 mcg/kg, repeated in 1-2 weeks. Two-dose regimen required to kill mites from newly hatched eggs. Efficacy 95% when given with topical permethrin; less effective as monotherapy. FDA-approved for scabies; particularly valuable for Norwegian scabies and mass outbreaks (institutional settings).

Management of Contacts and Environment

Critical Point: Treat all household members and sexual contacts simultaneously, even if asymptomatic, to prevent reinfestation. Failure to treat contacts major cause of treatment failure and recurrent scabies.

Fomites: Wash all bedding, clothing, and towels in hot water and dry on high heat. Items not washable placed in sealed bags for 2 weeks (mites cannot survive >3 days off skin). Vacuum carpets and furniture.

Treatment of Complications

Pruritus and Secondary Eczema: Topical corticosteroids (mid-potency) and antihistamines for pruritus relief during healing. Post-treatment pruritus may persist 2-4 weeks despite successful mite elimination.

Secondary Bacterial Infection: Treat with appropriate antibiotics (cephalexin or clindamycin). Reteat scabies if evident after antibiotic therapy.

Norwegian/Crusted Scabies

Severe form with thick crusts containing millions of mites. Presents in immunosuppressed (HIV/AIDS, post-transplant), elderly, or institutionalized patients. Highly contagious. Requires aggressive treatment: permethrin 5% cream applied more frequently (24-48 hours), oral ivermectin (200 mcg/kg on days 1, 2, 8, 9, 15), and topical keratolytics (salicylic acid, urea) to remove crusts.

References

  1. Currie BJ, et al. Scabies. Lancet. 2010;375(9715):1495-1502.
  2. Heukelbach J, et al. Scabies. Lancet Infect Dis. 2006;6(12):769-779.
  3. Strong M, et al. Global epidemiology of scabies. Curr Opin Infect Dis. 2016;29(2):109-118.
meta_description: Scabies: Sarcoptes scabiei mites. Permethrin 5% cream, ivermectin 200 mcg/kg (2 doses), treat all household contacts simultaneously to prevent reinfestation.

title: Lupus Skin Manifestations: Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Epidemiology and Clinical Significance

Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) affects 1 per 1000-10000 people globally, predominantly women of childbearing age. Skin manifestations occur in 50-85% of SLE patients and are often initial presenting symptoms. Acute cutaneous lupus affects 25-50% of SLE patients; chronic cutaneous lupus (discoid lupus) occurs in 20% of SLE and 5% of cutaneous lupus without systemic disease.

Pathophysiology

Type III hypersensitivity reaction (immune complex deposition) in skin produces inflammation. UV-B radiation increases cellular turnover and antigen presentation, triggering or exacerbating skin manifestations. Anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA) and anti-dsDNA antibodies characteristic of SLE; complement consumption (C3, C4) drives inflammation.

Acute Cutaneous Lupus

Malar Rash (Butterfly Rash): Most common (40% of SLE); erythematous, edematous plaques on cheeks and bridge of nose, typically sparing nasolabial folds. Fixed or photosensitive. Often accompanied by systemic symptoms (fever, arthralgia, malaise).

Photosensitive Lupus: Exacerbation of cutaneous lupus with UV exposure. Occurs in 40% of SLE patients.

Chronic Cutaneous Lupus (Discoid Lupus)

Hyperkeratotic plaques with follicular plugging on scalp, ears, and face. Violaceous erythema and hyperpigmentation at borders with central atrophy and scarring. Scarring alopecia common. Only 5-10% of discoid lupus progresses to systemic disease.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis supported by: (1) ANA serology (>95% sensitive for SLE), (2) anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith antibodies (specific for SLE), (3) low complement (C3, C4), (4) skin biopsy showing lichenoid interface dermatitis with immune complex deposition (immunofluorescence).

Treatment: Topical

Topical Corticosteroids: Mid-to-high potency agents (triamcinolone 0.1% cream, clobetasol 0.05%) applied twice daily. Efficacy 60-80% for acute lupus. Monitor for atrophy with prolonged use.

Calcineurin Inhibitors: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment effective alternative, particularly for facial involvement where steroid atrophy risk is concern.

Treatment: Systemic

Hydroxychloroquine: 200-400mg daily inhibits TLR activation and enhances regulatory T cell function. Requires baseline and annual ophthalmologic screening. Safe in pregnancy.

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day for severe cutaneous lupus; taper after clinical response (typically 2-4 weeks).

Immunosuppressants: Methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil for steroid-sparing effect in severe or refractory disease.

Photoprotection

Strict sun avoidance and broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ essential, as UV exposure triggers/exacerbates cutaneous lupus in nearly all patients. Protective clothing, hats, and UV-blocking window film minimize UV exposure.

References

  1. Kuhn A, et al. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus. J Dermatol. 2015;42(8):746-753.
  2. Tan EM, et al. The 1982 revised criteria for the classification of systemic lupus erythematosus. Arthritis Rheum. 1982;25(11):1271-1277.
meta_description: Lupus skin manifestations: malar rash, discoid lupus. Topical steroids, hydroxychloroquine, sunscreen (SPF 50+), systemic therapy if severe.

title: Dermatomyositis: What You Need to Know
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Dermatomyositis is an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy affecting skin and skeletal muscle (though skin manifestations can occur without muscle involvement in amyopathic form). Incidence 5.5 per million annually; typically affects women aged 40-60 years. 20-30% of patients have underlying malignancy; risk highest in older patients.

Pathophysiology

Autoimmune condition with characteristic antibodies (myositis-specific antibodies like anti-Mi2, anti-TIF1-γ, anti-NXP2). Complement-mediated microvasculitis with capillary destruction. CD8+ T cell infiltration of muscle and CD4+ infiltration of perimysial tissue. UVB exposure may trigger disease in genetically susceptible individuals.

Cutaneous Manifestations

Heliotrope Rash: Pathognomonic for dermatomyositis; violaceous (purple) erythema and edema of eyelids with "heliotrope" (violet) hue. Often accompanied by periorbital edema.

Gottron Papules: Violaceous, scaly papules on metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints (dorsal hands). Similar lesions on elbows and knees. Highly specific for dermatomyositis.

V-Sign and Shawl Sign: Erythematous patches on chest (V-sign) and shoulders/back (shawl sign); photosensitive distribution.

Poikiloderma: Hyperpigmentation, hypopigmentation, and atrophy creating variegated appearance; common in chronic disease.

Myositis Features

Proximal muscle weakness (hips, shoulders) developing insidiously over weeks to months. Dysphagia from pharyngeal/esophageal involvement (25%). Elevated creatine kinase (CK) 10-50x normal; aldolase also elevated. EMG shows myopathic pattern.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis supported by: (1) elevated muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase), (2) myositis-specific antibodies (anti-Mi2, anti-TIF1-γ, anti-NXP2), (3) skin biopsy showing interface dermatitis with capillary necrosis, (4) muscle biopsy/MRI showing inflammation, (5) EMG myopathic pattern.

Malignancy Screening

Dermatomyositis associated with malignancy in 20-30% of adult-onset cases. Screen at diagnosis and annually: chest imaging, abdominal/pelvic imaging, age/gender-appropriate cancer screening (colonoscopy, mammography, prostate). Anti-TIF1-γ and anti-NXP2 antibodies associated with higher malignancy risk.

Treatment: Skin Manifestations

Topical Corticosteroids: Mid-to-high potency agents for localized cutaneous disease. Efficacy limited; systemic therapy usually required.

Topical Calcineurin Inhibitors: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for facial dermatitis.

Hydroxychloroquine: 200-400mg daily provides modest benefit for cutaneous manifestations; safe for chronic use.

Treatment: Systemic

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisone 1-2mg/kg/day (up to 100mg daily) with gradual taper as clinical response achieved. Most patients require long-term therapy to prevent relapse.

Steroid-Sparing Agents: Methotrexate (7.5-25mg weekly) or mycophenolate mofetil (2-3g daily) reduce corticosteroid requirement and provide immunosuppression. Often initiated early alongside corticosteroids.

Refractory Cases: IV immunoglobulin (IVIG), azathioprine, or rituximab (off-label) for steroid-refractory disease.

Photoprotection

Strict UV avoidance and sunscreen SPF 50+ critical; UV exposure exacerbates cutaneous manifestations in nearly all patients. Protective clothing, wide-brimmed hats essential.

Amyopathic Dermatomyositis

Cutaneous manifestations without clinical or laboratory evidence of myositis (normal CK, EMG, and muscle biopsy). Represents 7-10% of dermatomyositis cases. Treated with sunscreen, topical agents, and hydroxychloroquine initially; systemic corticosteroids if severe or widespread skin involvement.

References

  1. Bohan A, et al. Polymyositis and dermatomyositis. N Engl J Med. 1975;292(7):344-347.
  2. Euwer RL, et al. Dermatologic manifestations of myositis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1994;31(3):391-398.
meta_description: Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, muscle weakness. Systemic corticosteroids, methotrexate, mycophenolate for immunosuppression.

title: Managing Pemphigus Vulgaris: Evidence-Based Approaches
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune blistering disorder with anti-desmoglein 3 (and anti-desmoglein 1 in some mucocutaneous cases) IgG antibodies causing acantholysis (loss of cell-to-cell adhesion). Incidence 0.5-4 per million annually; affects middle-aged and elderly primarily. Jewish, Mediterranean, and Indian ancestry at higher risk.

Pathophysiology

IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein 3 and/or desmoglein 1 (components of desmosomes connecting keratinocytes) bind to antigens causing loss of cell adhesion. Complement activation and phosphorylation of desmogleins lead to acantholysis. Loss of intact desmosomes results in flaccid intraepidermal blistering.

Clinical Features

Mucosal Involvement: Most common initial presentation (90% of patients). Erosions on buccal mucosa, palate, and tongue; painful, limiting eating and speaking. Erosions bleed easily; secondary infection risk.

Cutaneous Involvement: Flaccid blisters on erythematous base; rupture easily leaving painful erosions. Lesions typically appear on flexures, abdomen, and intertriginous areas. Positive Nikolsky sign (epidermal separation with gentle rubbing/pressure).

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis confirmed by: (1) histopathology showing intraepidermal acantholysis with "tombstone" appearance of basal cells, (2) direct immunofluorescence showing intercellular IgG deposition (pathognomonic), (3) serology with anti-desmoglein 3 and anti-desmoglein 1 IgG antibodies.

Treatment: Systemic Corticosteroids

First-Line: Prednisone 1-2mg/kg/day (typically 60-100mg) with gradual taper as clinical remission achieved. Takes 2-4 weeks to see response; slow taper over months to prevent relapse. Alternate-day dosing once-daily dosing reduced to 50% of previous dose (e.g., if on 60mg daily, alternate between 60mg one day and 30mg the next).

Long-Term Management: Most patients require prolonged therapy (years); discontinuation risks relapse in 50-70% of cases.

Steroid-Sparing Agents

Azathioprine: 1-2mg/kg/day allows corticosteroid dose reduction by 50% in many patients. Requires TPMT testing and baseline laboratory assessment.

Mycophenolate Mofetil: 2-3g daily in divided doses. Fewer drug interactions than azathioprine; emerging as preferred steroid-sparing agent.

Methotrexate: 7.5-25mg weekly for steroid-sparing effect, though less extensively studied than azathioprine/mycophenolate.

Biological Therapy

Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody targeting B cells and plasma cells. Multiple dosing regimens studied (375mg/m² weekly × 4 weeks, or 1000mg IV days 1 and 15). Emerging evidence supports efficacy for severe, steroid-refractory pemphigus. Potential for remission even after discontinuation of corticosteroids.

Topical Therapy

Topical Corticosteroids: High-potency agents (clobetasol 0.05% cream) applied to skin lesions twice daily. Minimal systemic absorption; useful adjunct to systemic therapy.

Mouth Rinses: Topical anesthetic rinses (viscous lidocaine) for oral pain relief. Antimicrobial rinses (chlorhexidine 0.12%) prevent secondary infection.

Management of Complications

Secondary Infections: Monitor for bacterial superinfection of erosions; use topical antibiotics or systemic antibiotics if systemic infection develops.

Steroid Side Effects: Osteoporosis prevention (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates), PCP prophylaxis if CD4 <200, GI protection (PPI for stress ulcer prophylaxis).

References

  1. Amagai M, et al. Pemphigus vulgaris and pemphigus foliaceus. J Clin Invest. 2002;109(1):26-33.
  2. Murrell DF, et al. Consensus statement on definitions of disease, end points, and therapeutic response for pemphigus. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2008;58(6):1043-1046.
meta_description: Pemphigus Vulgaris: autoimmune blistering disorder. Systemic corticosteroids 1-2mg/kg/day, steroid-sparing agents, biological therapies (rituximab).

title: Managing Granuloma Annulare: Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Granuloma annulare is a common benign inflammatory condition of unknown etiology affecting 0.1-0.4% of dermatology clinic patients. Bimodal age distribution with peaks in second and fifth decades; slight female predominance. Multiple variants exist (localized, generalized, subcutaneous, perforating). Approximately 75% of cases are localized; 15-25% generalized.

Pathophysiology

Histology shows palisading granulomas with lymphocytes and histiocytes surrounding area of necrobiosis. Etiology unknown; association with diabetes, tuberculosis, and sarcoidosis reported but uncommon. Trauma may trigger disease. CD4+ T cell infiltration suggests cell-mediated immune response.

Clinical Features

Localized Form: Small (1-3mm), firm papules forming annular (ring) configuration with central clearing. Skin-colored or erythematous. Common sites: dorsal hands, feet, shins. Mild pruritus or asymptomatic.

Generalized Form: Numerous papules/nodules (>10) scattered on trunk and extremities; may be annular, nonspecific, or nodular. More common in children and young adults; may be associated with systemic disease.

Subcutaneous Form: Painless nodules in subcutaneous tissues typically on lower extremities and buttocks; rare.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis confirmed by dermoscopy showing annular pattern and histopathology showing palisading granulomas. Direct immunofluorescence negative (distinguishes from sarcoidosis and other granulomatous diseases).

Natural History

Self-limited; 75% of localized cases resolve within 2 years without treatment. Generalized forms often persist longer, sometimes indefinitely. Recurrence after resolution occurs in 40% of cases. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation common.

Treatment: Observation

Watchful waiting appropriate given benign nature and high rate of spontaneous resolution. Reassure patients about self-limited course and favorable prognosis. Many patients prefer observation over active treatment.

Treatment: Topical

Topical Corticosteroids: Mid-to-high potency agents (triamcinolone 0.1% cream, clobetasol 0.05%) applied twice daily. Efficacy modest; slows progression but doesn't prevent recurrence.

Topical Calcineurin Inhibitors: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment alternative for facial involvement.

Treatment: Intralesional Corticosteroids

Intralesional Triamcinolone: 2.5-5mg/mL injected into lesions; repeated every 4-6 weeks. Efficacy 60-80% for localized disease. Most effective approach for limited number of lesions.

Treatment: Systemic

Phototherapy: NB-UVB (2-3 times weekly) effective for generalized disease; requires 12-20 weeks. Response rates 60-70%.

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day reserved for extensive, rapidly progressive, or symptomatic generalized disease. Usually tapered after 4-8 weeks.

Screening for Associated Conditions

No routine screening necessary for localized granuloma annulare. For generalized form, consider evaluation for diabetes (fasting glucose), tuberculosis (chest X-ray if risk factors), and sarcoidosis (ACE level, chest X-ray) in selected cases, though associations rare.

References

  1. Dabski K, et al. Granuloma annulare: Comparison of localized and systemic forms. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1992;27(3):319-324.
  2. O'Neill JL, et al. Granuloma annulare. Semin Cutan Med Surg. 2013;32(3-4):179-189.
meta_description: Granuloma Annulare: benign self-limited papules, often with central clearing. Topical steroids, intralesional corticosteroids, phototherapy, or observation.

title: Seborrheic Keratosis: Diagnosis and Management Strategies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Seborrheic keratosis is the most common benign skin growth in older adults. Affects 50-75% of adults >50 years; increases in prevalence with age. Etiology unknown; genetic predisposition likely. No malignant potential (non-melanocytic, non-keratinocytic origin). Multiple lesions common; some individuals develop hundreds.

Pathophysiology

Benign proliferation of basal keratinocytes within epidermis. Composed of solid nests and cords of basaloid cells without atypia. Accumulation of keratin produces characteristic appearance. No connection between seborrheic keratosis and sebaceous glands despite name.

Clinical Features

Characteristic "stuck-on" appearance; appear superficial as if pasted onto skin. Color ranges from tan to dark brown or black. Waxy, scaly surface often with central keratotic plug. Size varies (2mm to >3cm). Common sites: face, chest, back, axillae. Usually asymptomatic; irritation from friction possible (clothing, shaving).

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic morphology ("stuck-on" appearance, waxy surface). Dermoscopy shows comedo-like openings and milia-like cysts (pathognomonic). Biopsy unnecessary unless atypical features present (asymmetry, irregular borders, color variation).

Differential Diagnosis

Important Distinction from Melanoma: While seborrheic keratosis can be dark and large, it has benign features. Melanoma typically shows asymmetry, irregular borders, color variation, and rapid growth. "Ugly duckling" sign (lesion looks different from others) suggests melanoma; dermoscopy helps differentiate.

Treatment: Observation

No treatment necessary; lesions are benign and do not become malignant. However, many patients request removal for cosmetic reasons or because lesions are irritated by friction. Cryotherapy, curettage, or shave removal can be performed.

Treatment Options

Cryotherapy: Liquid nitrogen applied every 2-4 weeks. Efficacy good; modest pain/blistering. May require 2-3 treatments. Post-inflammatory hypopigmentation common in darker skin types.

Curettage: Sharp curette used to scrape lesion after local anesthesia. Highly effective; may leave small scar.

Shave Removal: Shave lesion flush with surrounding skin under local anesthesia. Simple office procedure; minimal scarring. Most cosmetically favorable option.

Patient Counseling

Reassurance about benign nature essential; many patients worry about malignancy. Explain no malignant potential and favorable prognosis. Discuss that new seborrheic keratosis lesions will likely develop with aging.

References

  1. Ackerman AB. Seborrheic keratosis is not seborrheic keratosis. Am J Dermatopathol. 2000;22(1):74-76.
  2. Braun RP, et al. Dermoscopy of seborrheic keratosis. Arch Dermatol. 2002;138(12):1556-1560.
meta_description: Seborrheic Keratosis: common benign skin growths. Cryotherapy, curettage, shave removal. Monitor appearance for malignant transformation. Reassure about benign nature.

title: Acne Mechanica: Sports Equipment and Friction-Induced Breakouts
content:
Definition and Pathophysiology

Acne mechanica develops from repetitive friction, pressure, or occlusion over affected skin areas. Primarily affects athletes but occurs with any activity producing friction (tight straps, uniforms, equipment). Friction increases follicular obstruction and sebum retention; sweat and moisture exacerbate condition. Cutibacterium acnes proliferation driven by heat, humidity, and reduced oxygen availability in occluded areas.

Clinical Presentation

Monomorphic papules and pustules localized to areas of friction: shoulders (sports bra/uniform straps), chest, back, thighs. Lesions spare usual acne distribution (face). Non-comedonal; predominantly inflammatory papules. Often painful or pruritic. Closely-timed relationship between equipment use and lesion appearance diagnostic.

Risk Factors and Triggers

Tight, occlusive clothing; non-breathable fabrics; sweat retention; poor hygiene; heat; sports equipment (helmets, shoulder pads, sports bras). Risk increases with duration and intensity of friction.

Prevention and Management

Friction Reduction: Loosen clothing, use moisture-wicking fabrics, apply protective barriers (lubricated gauze, athletic tape) under areas of friction.

Moisture Control: Change out of sweaty clothes immediately; shower within 15-30 minutes of activity. Moisture-wicking undergarments reduce sweat retention.

Topical Treatment: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5% applied twice daily to affected areas. Salicylic acid 2% products alternative. Lightweight, non-comedogenic moisturizers.

Systemic Treatment

Rarely necessary; topical measures usually sufficient. Oral antibiotics (doxycycline 100mg daily) for extensive disease unresponsive to topical therapy.

Prevention Strategy

Prevention superior to treatment; emphasis on friction reduction, moisture control, and regular showering after activity. Address equipment fit and breathability.

References

  1. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
  2. Schachner L, et al. Acne in athletes. Cutis. 2001;68(1):28-32.
meta_description: Acne Mechanica: friction-induced breakouts from sports equipment. Reduce friction, moisture control, topical benzoyl peroxide or salicylic acid.

title: Neonatal Acne: Understanding Newborn Skin Breakouts
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Neonatal acne occurs in approximately 20% of newborns during first 3 months of life. Results from exposure to maternal androgens transplacentally and in breast milk. Lesions typically appear within first 2-4 weeks of life; peak incidence around 3 weeks. Spontaneous resolution expected within 3-6 months in vast majority.

Pathophysiology

Maternal androgens (particularly testosterone, androstenedione) transplacentally transmitted stimulate fetal sebaceous glands. Postnatally, continued androgen exposure through breast milk and declining placental hormone transfer gradually resolve condition. No bacterial colonization required; purely hormonal etiology distinguishes neonatal acne from infantile acne.

Clinical Features

Small open and closed comedones predominantly on cheeks, nose, forehead, and chin. Pustules may develop but severe inflammatory lesions uncommon. Excoriation absent (unlike infantile acne). Lesions confined to face; no involvement of trunk or extremities. No systemic symptoms.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on age of onset (within first 3 months), location (face), and morphology. Distinction from infantile acne (onset 3-16 weeks, more inflammatory, potential for scarring) important for prognostic counseling.

Natural History

Self-limited; expected resolution within 3-6 months as maternal androgens clear. No scarring potential given purely hormonal etiology and absence of severe inflammation. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation rare; resolves within weeks.

Management

Reassurance: Most important aspect; explain benign, self-limited nature to concerned parents.

Gentle Cleansing: Wash face gently once or twice daily with mild cleanser and water. Avoid overwashing or vigorous scrubbing.

Avoid Topical Products: Generally not recommended; not absorbed systemically, but potential for irritation in sensitive infant skin. Avoid any products containing salicylic acid, benzoyl peroxide, or retinoids in neonatal period.

Monitoring: Follow-up at 1-2 months to reassess. Expected improvement visible by 3-6 months.

When to Treat

Treatment rarely indicated given self-limited nature. Only if lesions remarkably extensive or parents request treatment: gentle topical clindamycin lotion (minimal absorption) or mild topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025%) applied sparingly. Avoid systemic medications in neonates.

References

  1. Niamba P, et al. The epidemiology of neonatal acne. J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2013;27(1):3-10.
  2. Del Rosso JQ, et al. Newborn and infantile acne. Pediatr Dermatol. 2003;20(5):431-435.
meta_description: Neonatal Acne: transient acne in newborns from maternal androgens. Usually self-resolves. Gentle cleansing only; rarely need treatment.

title: Acne Conglobata: Severe Interconnected Nodular Acne
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Acne conglobata is a rare, severe form of acne vulgaris (0.1-0.3% of acne cases) characterized by large interconnected nodules and abscesses. Predominantly affects men in their 20s-30s. Highly scarring; devastating psychosocial impact. Often represents severe end of acne spectrum or may develop de novo.

Pathophysiology

Extreme inflammatory response to Cutibacterium acnes and follicular rupture. Coalescence of adjacent lesions produces large, interconnected nodules and draining sinuses. Secondary bacterial infection common. Genetic predisposition to heightened inflammatory response drives severe phenotype.

Clinical Features

Large (>5mm), firm, interconnected nodules and abscesses coalescing into larger masses. Draining sinuses and tracts with purulent drainage common. Severe pain and inflammation. Predominant involvement: chest, back, shoulders, buttocks. Post-inflammatory scars inevitable given severity.

Complications

Severe, extensive scarring (atrophic, hypertrophic, keloid) in 80-100% of cases. Secondary infection with potential for systemic spread. Psychosocial impact profound; depression and social withdrawal common.

Treatment: Systemic Antibiotics

Doxycycline 100mg twice daily or minocycline 100mg twice daily combined with topical benzoyl peroxide. Modest efficacy; never monotherapy.

Treatment: Isotretinoin

Indicated for: Nearly all patients with acne conglobata given severe scarring risk and poor response to conventional therapy.

Dosing: 0.5-1.0mg/kg/day with cumulative target 120-150mg/kg. Standard treatment duration 15-20 weeks.

Efficacy: 90% achieve long-term remission or cure; relapse rare. Prevents progressive scarring and allows healing of existing lesions.

Adjunctive Therapy

Systemic Corticosteroids: Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day for 1-4 weeks during acute flares rapidly reduces inflammation and pain. Taper over 2-4 weeks to avoid rebound.

Intralesional Corticosteroids: Triamcinolone acetonide 2.5-5mg/mL injected into active lesions rapidly reduces inflammation.

Scar Revision

After isotretinoin completion and disease stabilization, scar revision pursued: subcision, punch grafting, laser resurfacing (fractional CO2, erbium), or microneedling. Timing: 6-12 months after isotretinoin completion.

Psychosocial Support

Mental health referral essential; significant depression/anxiety warrant professional intervention. Support groups and cosmetic counseling improve outcomes.

References

  1. Goulden V, et al. Acne fulminans and acne conglobata. Dermatology. 1995;190(3):229-234.
  2. Cordain L, et al. Acne vulgaris: A disease of Western civilization. Arch Dermatol. 2002;138(12):1584-1590.
meta_description: Acne Conglobata: severe interconnected nodules, potential scarring. Isotretinoin 0.5-1mg/kg, oral antibiotics, systemic corticosteroids for acute flares.

title: Acne Keloidalis Nuchae: Bumps on the Back of the Neck
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Acne keloidalis nuchae (AKN), also termed folliculitis keloidalis, is a chronic scarring folliculitis affecting nape of neck. Predominantly affects African American men (prevalence 7-65% in this population); rare in other groups. Onset typically late teens to early 30s. Results from combination of folliculitis and abnormal wound healing (keloid formation).

Pathophysiology

Folliculitis triggered by: (1) close-clipped or curly hair piercing epidermis (pseudofolliculitis), (2) friction from collar, (3) poor hygiene, (4) secondary bacterial infection. Genetic predisposition to abnormal collagen deposition produces keloid-like hypertrophic scars. Inflammatory cascade perpetuates lesion progression.

Clinical Features

Small, firm, hyperpigmented or erythematous papules and nodules on nape of neck in area of close-clipped hair. Lesions coalesce into larger hypertrophic/keloid scars with pain and pruritus. Draining pustules and tracts. Progressive course if untreated; can lead to significant scarring and alopecia.

Early Stage Management

Hair Management: Avoid close clipping; allow longer hair or use clippers with wider guards (#2 or #3 instead of #0 or #1). Avoid shaving; depilatory creams alternative. Some benefit from laser hair removal to permanently reduce hair.

Topical Treatment: Topical antibiotics (clindamycin, erythromycin) applied twice daily. Mid-potency corticosteroid creams (triamcinolone 0.1%) applied twice daily.

Oral Antibiotics: Doxycycline 100mg daily or minocycline 100mg daily for 12+ weeks reduces inflammation and bacterial load.

Intralesional Therapy

Triamcinolone acetonide 10-40mg/mL injected into nodules every 4-6 weeks. Gradual flattening of hypertrophic scars. Often combined with oral antibiotics for enhanced efficacy.

Advanced/Resistant Disease

Surgical Excision: Complete surgical excision of affected area with primary closure or skin grafting. Success rates 70-90%; recurrence possible if underlying folliculitis not controlled. Best performed after achieving disease quiescence with medical therapy.

Laser Treatment: CO2 or erbium laser resurfacing may improve appearance but carries recurrence risk given chronic underlying folliculitis.

Maintenance

Long-term maintenance with hair removal (depilatory, laser, or longer clipper settings) and periodic intralesional steroid injections prevent recurrence.

References

  1. Ogunbiyi AO, et al. Acne keloidalis nuchae. Clin Exp Dermatol. 2016;41(8):874-881.
  2. Perkins W, et al. Pseudofolliculitis barbae. Dermatol Clin. 2014;32(2):183-191.
meta_description: Acne Keloidalis Nuchae: folliculitis on nape, keloid scars. Topical/intralesional steroids, oral antibiotics, surgical excision for severe cases.

title: Chloracne: Industrial Chemical-Induced Acne
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Chloracne is a severe follicular disease caused by exposure to halogenated aromatic hydrocarbons, particularly polychlorinated dibenzodioxins (dioxins), dibenzofurans, and biphenyls. Occurs in occupational settings (chemical manufacturing, waste incinerators, thermal decomposition). Notable historical exposure: Seveso disaster (1976); Vietnam veterans exposed to Agent Orange dioxin contaminant.

Pathophysiology

Dioxins and related compounds activate aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathways triggering keratinocyte proliferation, sebaceous gland alteration, and profound inflammation. Dose-dependent; severity correlates with exposure level. Systemic absorption causes widespread effects beyond skin (hepatotoxicity, immunosuppression, endocrine disruption).

Clinical Features

Severe comedonal and papular acne with predominantly closed comedones (milium-like lesions). Facial involvement predominant, though can affect trunk and extremities. Associated follicular hyperkeratosis and pilosebaceous inflammation. Often monomorphic with uniform lesion appearance. Severe pruritus and pain common.

Systemic Manifestations

Beyond skin: hepatomegaly and abnormal liver function (elevation in liver enzymes), altered lipid metabolism, immune suppression (reduced T cell function), endocrine disruption (reproductive toxicity), and increased cancer risk with chronic exposure.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic acneiform eruption following documented chemical exposure. Confirmation: serum dioxin levels (exceptionally expensive and not routinely performed). Histopathology shows follicular hyperkeratinization and inflammation.

Management

Primary Intervention: Remove from exposure source immediately. Cessation of exposure prerequisite for resolution; continued exposure prevents healing.

Supportive Skin Care: Gentle cleansing with non-irritating cleansers. Avoid extracting comedones (high risk of scarring). Emollients provide barrier support.

Topical Therapy: Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025-0.1%) can improve comedone formation and follicular plugging. Start low concentration given severe irritation risk. Benzoyl peroxide less effective than in typical acne.

Systemic Therapy: Limited efficacy; oral retinoids (isotretinoin, acitretin) reserved for severe, unresponsive cases. Requires careful monitoring given systemic toxicity already present from dioxin exposure.

Medical Surveillance

Comprehensive medical evaluation at exposure documentation: baseline liver function tests, lipid panel, immune function assessment, reproductive assessment if applicable. Long-term follow-up given delayed manifestation of dioxin toxicity. Cancer surveillance per occupational exposure guidelines.

Prognosis

Slow resolution after exposure cessation (may take months to years for complete clearance). Residual post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and atrophic scars common. Systemic health effects may persist indefinitely.

References

  1. Calvert GM, et al. Evaluating noncancer health effects of dioxin exposure. Scand J Work Environ Health. 1997;23(Suppl 3):54-62.
  2. Jones KC, et al. Human exposure to persistent organic pollutants. Environ Int. 2005;31(2):209-218.
meta_description: Chloracne: severe acne from dioxin/chemical exposure. Remove exposure source, topical retinoids, supportive care, medical surveillance for health effects.

title: Acne Excoriée: When Picking Makes Acne Worse
content:
Definition and Pathophysiology

Acne excoriée (also acne excoriée des jeunes filles) refers to acne lesions that become severely damaged through repetitive picking, scratching, or manipulation by the patient. Often associated with body-focused repetitive behaviors (BFRBs), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), anxiety, or dermatillomania. Mechanical trauma produces larger, deeper lesions at risk for secondary infection and severe scarring.

Epidemiology

Predominantly affects adolescent girls and young women (hence historical name), though increasingly recognized in men. Often begins with mild acne that patient exacerbates through picking. Associated with perfectionism, anxiety, stress, and body image concerns.

Clinical Features

Acne lesions with evidence of picking: erosions, crusts, excoriations, scabs. Lesions typically worse than underlying acne severity would suggest. Distribution often shows more trauma on accessible areas (face, chest) versus less accessible sites. Secondary infection common. Significant scarring potential from repeated trauma.

Psychological Assessment

Screen for underlying anxiety, OCD, depression. Assess triggers: stress, anxiety, boredom. Determine if picking is conscious or unconscious/habitual. Evaluate impact on self-esteem and psychosocial functioning. Referral for mental health evaluation appropriate if significant psychiatric component identified.

Dermatologic Treatment

Treat Underlying Acne: Benzoyl peroxide 5%, topical retinoids, or oral antibiotics address acne. Reducing acne burden decreases picking triggers.

Topical Corticosteroids: Low-to-mid potency (hydrocortisone 1%, triamcinolone 0.1%) applied to excoriated areas reduces inflammation and pruritus, decreasing picking urge.

Barrier Protection: Occlusive dressings (hydrocolloid patches, bandages) over excoriated lesions prevent access for picking. Often surprisingly effective behavioral intervention.

Behavioral Interventions

Habit Reversal Training: Identify triggers and substitution behaviors. Alternative tension-releasing activities: stress ball, fidget toy, hand clenching, exercise.

Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT): Address underlying anxiety, perfectionism, body image concerns. Helpful for conscious picking driven by anxiety/stress.

Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT): Acceptance of urge without acting on it; mindfulness-based approaches.

Pharmacotherapy

For OCD-related picking: selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) like sertraline (50-200mg daily) or paroxetine. Augmentation with medications targeting anxiety may benefit. Dermatologist-psychiatrist collaboration optimal.

Monitoring and Prognosis

Combination of dermatologic treatment, behavioral modification, and mental health support most effective. However, recurrence of picking behavior common if underlying anxiety/OCD not adequately addressed. Long-term psychosocial support and periodic dermatologic follow-up recommended.

References

  1. Flessner CA, et al. Skin picking disorder. J Clin Psychol. 2009;65(12):1270-1280.
  2. Grant JE, et al. Skin picking disorder. Am J Psychiatry. 2012;169(11):1143-1149.
meta_description: Acne Excoriée: self-inflicted skin trauma from picking acne lesions. Behavioral modification, topical steroids, psychotherapy for underlying anxiety/OCD.

title: Steroid Acne: Corticosteroid-Induced Breakouts
content:
Definition and Pathophysiology

Steroid acne (corticosteroid-induced acne) develops from systemic or topical corticosteroid use. Mechanism: corticosteroids increase sebum production, promote follicular hyperkeratinization, and suppress local immunity. Higher potency and prolonged duration increase risk. Paradoxically, corticosteroids prescribed for inflammatory skin conditions can induce or worsen acne.

Epidemiology

Occurs in approximately 5-15% of patients on systemic corticosteroids. Risk increases with: higher doses, prolonged duration, intralesional steroid injections (particularly for acne lesions). Appears within 2-12 weeks of steroid initiation.

Clinical Features

Distinctive Features: Monomorphic papules and pustules (uniform in appearance) on face, trunk, and proximal extremities. Notable absence of comedones (unlike typical acne). Often severe pruritus. May progress to inflammatory nodules if severe.

Distribution: Face (particularly around mouth and chin), chest, back, axillae. Typically affects areas previously clear.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on: (1) temporal relationship with corticosteroid initiation, (2) monomorphic papular-pustular eruption, (3) involvement of typically-clear areas, (4) response to steroid reduction/discontinuation. Histopathology shows folliculitis without comedones.

Prevention

When corticosteroids necessary, use lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Frequent monitoring for acne development during therapy. Patient education regarding acne risk.

Management

Steroid Modification: Reduce dose or discontinue if clinically possible. Taper gradually to avoid rebound disease exacerbation. Best single intervention; acne typically resolves within 2-4 weeks of dose reduction or discontinuation.

Topical Therapy: While on necessary corticosteroids, concurrent use of anti-acne agents may minimize flares. Benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5% twice daily or topical retinoids (tretinoin, adapalene) can reduce lesion formation.

Oral Antibiotics: Doxycycline 100mg daily for moderate-severe steroid-induced acne unresponsive to topical therapy. Continue until steroid dose significantly reduced.

Intralesional Steroid Caution

Intralesional corticosteroid injections for inflammatory lesions paradoxically increase risk of subsequent steroid acne. Alternative: intralesional acne-targeting agents (5-fluorouracil) or systemic anti-acne therapy during steroid-required period.

References

  1. Ciarrocca K, et al. Corticosteroid-induced acne. Dermatol Surg. 2004;30(2):287-291.
  2. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
meta_description: Steroid Acne: from topical/systemic corticosteroids. Monomorphic papules on face, trunk. Discontinue steroid if possible, topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide.

title: Infantile Acne: Persistent Acne in Babies
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Infantile acne occurs in infants aged 3-16 weeks of life. Distinct from neonatal acne (onset <3 weeks, primarily from maternal androgens). Infantile acne results from combination of maternal androgens, sebaceous gland activity, and Cutibacterium acnes colonization. Occurs in approximately 0.5-2% of infants; slightly more common in males.

Pathophysiology

Unlike purely hormonally-mediated neonatal acne, infantile acne involves bacterial colonization combined with androgenic stimulation. Sebaceous glands remain enlarged and active. Lesions typically more inflammatory than neonatal acne with potential for scarring if neglected.

Clinical Features

Open and closed comedones predominantly on cheeks and nose, but papules and pustules more common than in neonatal acne. Lesions may become inflamed with risk of scarring. Excoriations from irritation or parental manipulation worsen inflammation. Trunk and extremity involvement more common than in neonatal acne.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on age of onset (3-16 weeks), morphology (comedones + inflammatory papules), and location (face predominant but may involve trunk). Distinction from neonatal acne important for prognostic counseling and determining need for evaluation.

Evaluation for Androgen Excess

Most infantile acne is idiopathic and self-resolving. However, severe/persistent infantile acne (>6 months, extensive involvement, significant scarring) warrants evaluation for androgen excess: measure free testosterone, DHEA-S, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (to screen for congenital adrenal hyperplasia, 1-3% of severe infantile acne). Imaging (ultrasound, MRI) if laboratory abnormalities identified.

Management

Reassurance: Most important; explain expected resolution within 3-6 months to alleviate parental anxiety.

Gentle Skin Care: Wash face once or twice daily with mild cleanser and water. Avoid aggressive scrubbing or touching lesions.

Minimize Products: Avoid unnecessary creams, oils, powders that may occlude follicles.

Avoid Topical Treatments: Most topical acne medications not studied in infants <3 months. Salicylic acid, benzoyl peroxide, retinoids generally avoided due to irritation and absorption risk.

Monitoring: Follow-up at 4-8 weeks. Expect gradual improvement over 3-6 months.

Treatment for Severe/Persistent Cases

If infantile acne persists beyond 6 months or becomes severe with scarring risk: gentle topical clindamycin lotion twice daily (minimal absorption). Systemic antibiotics rarely indicated in infants. Address any identified hormonal abnormalities.

References

  1. Niamba P, et al. The epidemiology of neonatal acne. J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2013;27(1):3-10.
  2. Del Rosso JQ. Newborn and infantile acne. Pediatr Dermatol. 2003;20(5):431-435.
meta_description: Infantile Acne: persistent acne in babies 3-16 weeks old. Usually self-resolving. Evaluate for androgen excess if severe. Gentle cleansing; avoid topical treatments.

title: Acne Fulminans: Acute Ulcerative Acne Emergency
content:
Definition and Epidemiology

Acne fulminans is the most severe form of acne vulgaris, characterized by acute, explosive onset of severe nodular acne with systemic manifestations. Rare (0.1% of acne cases). Predominantly affects adolescent males. Often associated with isotretinoin initiation or withdrawal (particularly with concurrent systemic corticosteroids taper). Can occur spontaneously without preceding acne.

Pathophysiology

Severe abnormal immune response to Cutibacterium acnes with intense neutrophilic and lymphocytic infiltration. Follicular rupture produces extensive tissue necrosis and abscess formation. Systemic IL-6 elevation correlates with severity. Testosterone surge during puberty and genetic predisposition important cofactors.

Clinical Features

Skin Manifestations: Sudden eruption of large, hemorrhagic, often ulcerative nodules and cysts coalescing into extensive inflammatory plaques. Draining sinuses with purulent discharge. Predominantly on chest, shoulders, and back; face less affected. Severe pain, tenderness, and edema.

Systemic Symptoms: Fever (often >38.5°C), malaise, headache, arthralgia (particularly knees and elbows), myalgia. Hepatosplenomegaly possible. Constitutional symptoms profound; patients severely ill.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on acute onset of severe ulcerative nodular acne with systemic symptoms. Elevated inflammatory markers: ESR elevated, CRP elevated, WBC often elevated (12,000-16,000). Chest imaging (X-ray, CT) if respiratory symptoms or pulmonary involvement suspected. Severe cases may present with sepsis-like picture requiring ICU admission.

Management: Systemic Corticosteroids

First-Line Therapy: Systemic corticosteroids are absolutely indicated and dramatically improve acne fulminans. Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day (typically 40-80mg daily) for 1-4 weeks with gradual taper. Rapid improvement visible within 7-10 days; systemic symptoms resolve first, cutaneous lesions more gradual.

Dosing and Taper: Cannot be abruptly discontinued; slow taper over 2-4 weeks prevents rebound exacerbation. Typical approach: high-dose for 1-2 weeks, then reduce by 10% every 3-5 days.

Management: Isotretinoin

Timing: Initiate isotretinoin after systemic corticosteroids achieve disease control (typically 2-4 weeks of steroid therapy). Concurrent isotretinoin + systemic corticosteroids at high doses increases adverse effects risk; sequential therapy preferable.

Dosing: Standard acne conglobata dosing: 0.5-1mg/kg/day with cumulative target 120-150mg/kg. Essential therapy; prevents recurrence and halts progression to lifelong scarring.

Supportive Care

Pain management: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, opioids for severe cases. Cool compresses reduce discomfort. Psychological support essential given acute severity and distress. Nutritional support may be needed if systemic involvement severe.

Complications

Secondary bacterial infection common; broad-spectrum antibiotics if signs of infection. Arthritis develops in some cases (particularly knee arthritis); typically resolves with steroid therapy. Permanent hypertrophic/keloid scarring in 80%+ of cases despite treatment; requires scar revision later.

Prognosis

With appropriate systemic corticosteroid therapy followed by isotretinoin, most patients achieve disease control. However, extensive permanent scarring is common and requires revision procedures (subcision, laser, microneedling) 6-12 months after disease resolution. Psychosocial recovery may take years.

References

  1. Goulden V, et al. Acne fulminans and acne conglobata. Dermatology. 1995;190(3):229-234.
  2. Walsh LJ. Acne fulminans. Australas J Dermatol. 2001;42(3):165-166.
meta_description: Acne Fulminans: severe, ulcerative acne with systemic symptoms. Medical emergency. Systemic corticosteroids 0.5-1mg/kg, isotretinoin after disease control.

title: Gram-Negative Folliculitis: Antibiotic-Resistant Acne
content:
Definition and Pathophysiology

Gram-negative folliculitis is an uncommon form of antibiotic-resistant folliculitis caused by gram-negative bacteria (most commonly Klebsiella pneumoniae, Serratia marcescens, Proteus species, Pseudomonas aeruginosa) colonizing facial skin. Develops after prolonged acne antibiotic therapy (typically oral tetracyclines for 6+ months) due to selection for resistant gram-negative flora normally suppressed by oral antibiotics. Represents approximately 2-3% of refractory acne cases.

Clinical Presentation

Sudden worsening of acne on background of apparent improvement or disease control with antibiotics. Inflammatory pustules and papules, sometimes larger lesions. Predominant facial involvement. Lesions often drain purulent material. Clinical appearance similar to bacterial acne but resistant to standard therapy. Distinguishing feature: onset or worsening after extended antibiotic course.

Diagnosis

Clinical suspicion based on: (1) acne worsening after prolonged antibiotic therapy, (2) failure to respond to increased antibiotic doses, (3) inflammatory papules/pustules with purulent drainage. Confirmation: bacterial culture of pustular material showing gram-negative organisms. Gram stain/culture may show mixed flora.

Prevention

Limit duration of oral acne antibiotics: maximum 12 weeks therapy. Combined use with topical retinoids and benzoyl peroxide allows earlier antibiotic discontinuation. Proper antibiotic selection (doxycycline, minocycline with broad anti-inflammatory effects) and dosing essential.

Management

Discontinue Oral Antibiotics: Critical first step; continued antibiotics perpetuate dysbiosis favoring gram-negative overgrowth. Abrupt discontinuation typically safe; rebound acne may occur in some patients.

Topical Therapy: Continue topical benzoyl peroxide, retinoids, or clindamycin to suppress gram-negative flora and manage inflammatory lesions. Zinc pyrithione or topical antimicrobial agents show some benefit.

Systemic Isotretinoin: Appropriate for extensive, severe gram-negative folliculitis unresponsive to above measures. Dosing standard: 0.5-1mg/kg/day with cumulative target 120-150mg/kg. Curative; prevents recurrence.

Treatment Duration

Once oral antibiotics discontinued, gram-negative folliculitis typically improves within 2-4 weeks. Topical therapy continued for 12+ weeks as disease resolves. Close monitoring essential as initial worsening possible.

Prevention of Recurrence

Avoid prolonged antibiotic therapy in future. Utilize topical retinoids and benzoyl peroxide as first-line maintenance therapy. If systemic antibiotics necessary, limit duration to 12 weeks maximum and combine with topical agents for synergy.

References

  1. Norris JF, et al. Gram-negative folliculitis in patients treated with isotretinoin. Arch Dermatol. 1988;124(3):370-373.
  2. Leyden JJ, et al. Gram-negative folliculitis of the face. Cutis. 1983;32(2):148-151.
meta_description: Gram-Negative Folliculitis: resistant folliculitis from prolonged antibiotics. Klebsiella, Proteus, Serratia species. Discontinue antibiotics, oral isotretinoin, or topical agents.

title: Pomade Acne: Hair Product-Related Breakouts
content:
Definition and Pathophysiology

Pomade acne is comedonal and inflammatory acne resulting from application of occlusive hair products (pomades, oils, creams, waxes) to scalp and hairline areas. More common in individuals with curly/textured hair due to higher frequency and concentration of product application. Occlusion of follicles by product combined with friction from hair causes follicular obstruction and bacterial overgrowth.

Epidemiology

Predominantly affects individuals of African descent due to prevalence of textured hair requiring heavy styling products. Affects men and women; more common in men with pomade-heavy styling habits.

Clinical Features

Comedones and papules/pustules on hairline (frontal, temporal), scalp, and neck—areas in contact with hair products. Distribution corresponds to product application pattern. Predominantly comedonal; inflammatory component variable. Often associated with folliculitis keloidalis nuchae on nape if pomade applied heavily to this area.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on: (1) acne distribution over hair-bearing areas, (2) history of pomade/heavy product use, (3) improvement with product change/discontinuation.

Treatment

Product Modification: Primary intervention. Switch to non-comedogenic, lightweight hair products. Look for products labeled "non-comedogenic" or "oil-free." Water-based styling products preferable to oil-based pomades.

Hair Care Practices: Reduce quantity of product applied. Remove product nightly with gentle shampoo; some sleep in cotton caps to reduce product transfer to pillow/face. Limit styling manipulation.

Topical Acne Therapy: While transitioning products, apply benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5% or salicylic acid 2% to affected areas nightly. Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025-0.05%) helpful for comedones.

Mild Topical Corticosteroids: For inflammatory component, triamcinolone 0.1% cream applied to affected areas.

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate product modification. Lesions typically improve within 4-6 weeks of switching to non-comedogenic products combined with topical therapy. Recurrence prevented by maintaining non-comedogenic product selection long-term.

Prevention

Public education regarding comedogenic hair products and pomade acne development. Recommendation of non-comedogenic alternatives for individuals with predisposition (textured hair, frequent pomade use).

References

  1. Goh CL. Pomade acne. Cutis. 1993;51(3):199-202.
  2. Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945-973.
meta_description: Pomade Acne: from occlusive hair products. Non-comedogenic products, remove product nightly, topical retinoids and benzoyl peroxide treatment.

title: Occupational Acne: Workplace Chemical Exposure and Skin
content:
Occupational Acne is a condition that dermatologists encounter frequently in clinical practice. This comprehensive guide covers everything patients need to know about diagnosis, treatment options, and long-term management strategies.

Dermatological research continues to advance our understanding of occupational acne, leading to improved diagnostic techniques and more effective therapeutic interventions. Board-certified dermatologists play a crucial role in managing this condition.

Understanding Occupational Acne

Occupational Acne encompasses a spectrum of clinical presentations that vary in severity and impact on quality of life. The condition may present differently across age groups, skin types, and geographic regions, making individualized assessment critical.

Recent advances in dermatological science have significantly improved our understanding of the pathophysiology underlying occupational acne. These insights have led to the development of targeted therapies that address the root causes rather than merely managing symptoms.

Causes and Risk Factors

Multiple factors contribute to the development of occupational acne, including genetic predisposition, environmental triggers, immune system dysfunction, and lifestyle influences. Understanding these risk factors helps guide prevention strategies.

  • Genetic factors: Family history and inherited susceptibility play significant roles in disease development and progression
  • Environmental triggers: UV exposure, allergens, irritants, climate changes, and pollution can initiate or exacerbate the condition
  • Immune dysfunction: Abnormal immune responses, including both overactive and underactive immune pathways, contribute to disease pathology
  • Hormonal influences: Endocrine changes during puberty, pregnancy, menopause, and hormonal therapy may affect disease activity
  • Lifestyle factors: Diet, stress, sleep quality, and skincare habits can influence disease onset and severity

Signs and Symptoms

Clinical manifestations of occupational acne can range from mild to severe and may evolve over time. Early recognition of symptoms enables prompt intervention and better outcomes.

Patients should monitor for changes in skin appearance, texture, sensation, and any associated systemic symptoms. Documentation of symptom patterns, triggers, and response to treatments aids in clinical decision-making.

Diagnosis and Evaluation

Accurate diagnosis of occupational acne requires a thorough clinical examination, detailed patient history, and potentially supplementary diagnostic tests. Dermatologists use a combination of visual assessment, dermoscopy, and laboratory investigations to confirm the diagnosis.

Differential diagnosis is important as several conditions may present with similar features. A systematic approach to evaluation ensures appropriate treatment selection and avoids unnecessary interventions.

Treatment Options

Modern treatment approaches for occupational acne encompass a wide range of therapeutic modalities tailored to disease severity, patient preferences, and individual response patterns.

  • Topical therapies: First-line treatments applied directly to affected skin areas for localized disease control
  • Systemic medications: Oral or injectable treatments for moderate-to-severe cases requiring whole-body disease management
  • Procedural interventions: In-office procedures including laser therapy, light therapy, cryotherapy, and surgical approaches
  • Biologic therapies: Targeted immunomodulators that address specific inflammatory pathways involved in disease pathogenesis
  • Combination approaches: Multi-modal treatment plans that leverage synergistic effects of different therapeutic classes

Prevention and Self-Care

Preventive measures and appropriate self-care practices play a vital role in managing occupational acne and reducing flare frequency. Patient education about trigger avoidance and skin protection is a cornerstone of comprehensive management.

Regular follow-up with a dermatologist ensures treatment optimization and early detection of any disease progression or complications. Establishing a consistent skincare routine appropriate for the condition helps maintain skin health between office visits.

When to See a Dermatologist
