  5. Kaur R, et al. Dermatophyte infections. Int J Microbiol. 2008;2008:424186. PMID: 18392088
  6. Nenoff P, et al. Dermatophytes and non-dermatophyte fungi. Clin Dermatol. 2010;28(4):408-412. PMID: 20797510
  7. Acharyya S, et al. Tinea capitis in children. Indian J Dermatol Venereol Leprol. 2014;80(6):522-529. PMID: 25382559
  8. Havlickova B, et al. The epidemiology of dermatophytoses. Curr Opin Infect Dis. 2008;21(2):94-102. PMID: 18317035
meta_description: Tinea capitis affects 3-15% of children worldwide. Learn about Microsporum/Trichophyton species, oral antifungal therapy, and contagion control.

title: Sporotrichosis: The Rose Gardener's Disease
content:
Understanding Sporotrichosis

Sporotrichosis, caused by the dimorphic fungus Sporothrix schenckii, is a chronic cutaneous and lymphatic infection typically acquired through traumatic inoculation from plant material (rose thorns, hay, sphagnum moss) or soil. The condition characterizes "rose gardener's disease" due to classic occupational association. Sporotrichosis presents with initial chancre-like ulcer at inoculation site followed by lymphangitis and nodular lymphangitis tracking proximally along draining lymphatics. Systemic sporotrichosis occurs rarely in immunocompromised individuals. The infection persists for months-years without treatment, gradually worsening with potential for functional impairment and permanent scarring.

Epidemiology

Sporotrichosis occurs worldwide with higher prevalence in subtropical/tropical regions (1-5 per million population). Most cases result from occupational exposure (gardeners, farmers, florists, laborers). Peak age 40-60 years. Sporothrix schenckii exists ubiquitously in soil and plants; infection requires traumatic inoculation. Occupational exposure increases risk 1000-fold in soil workers versus general population.

Clinical Presentation

Initial presentation (2-12 weeks post-inoculation) includes indurated ulcer or papule at inoculation site (typically fingers, hands, forearms where thorns penetrate). Progressive nodular lymphangitis develops, with characteristic string-like chain of nodular lesions ascending toward regional lymph nodes. Approximately 90% of cases show lymphangitic presentation; 10% show disseminated cutaneous involvement. Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) occur in 20-30% of patients. Untreated disease persists for months-years without spontaneous resolution.

Diagnosis

Fungal culture of ulcer fluid/tissue confirms Sporothrix schenckii. Histology shows chronic inflammation with fungal organisms (rarely visible). Yeast antigen detection supports diagnosis. Sporotrichin skin test positive in 80-90% of infected patients.

Treatment

Itraconazole 200 mg daily for 3-6 months achieves cure in 90-95% of lymphangitic sporotrichosis. Alternative: potassium iodide (SSKI) 1 gram three times daily escalating to tolerance, achieves 50-60% cure rates in 4-6 weeks but more side effects. Terbinafine 500 mg daily shows emerging efficacy (80-85%) in limited studies. Systemic disease requires prolonged therapy (12+ months) with potential lifelong suppressive therapy in severe immunocompromised patients. Surgical intervention may be needed for extensive tissue destruction.

Prognosis

With itraconazole therapy, 90-95% achieve cure within 3-6 months. Relapses rare (<5%) with complete therapy. Occupational awareness and protective measures (gloves, wound care) prevent infection in exposed workers.

References

  1. Kauffman CA. Sporotrichosis. Clin Infect Dis. 1999;29(2):231-236. PMID: 10476722
  2. Rippon JW. Medical Mycology. 3rd ed. W.B. Saunders; 1988. 407-447
  3. Hay RJ. Fungal infections. Dermatol Clin. 2018;36(3):189-198. PMID: 29935588
  4. Kontoyiannis DP, et al. Sporotrichosis. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006;20(3):607-618. PMID: 16962509
  5. Bonifaz A, et al. Sporotrichosis. Clin Dermatol. 2012;30(4):437-443. PMID: 22682193
  6. Mares DJ. Dimorphic fungal pathogens. Clin Lab Med. 2015;35(2):339-359. PMID: 26004450
  7. Moyes RB. Sporothrix schenckii. Cutis. 2009;84(3):119-123. PMID: 19830254
  8. Sharkey-Mathis PK. Sporotrichosis. Semin Dermatol. 1997;16(4):309-314. PMID: 9421606
meta_description: Sporotrichosis is a fungal infection from rose thorns and plants. Learn about itraconazole treatment and lymphangitis complications.

title: Majocchi Granuloma: Deep Follicular Fungal Infection
content:
Understanding Majocchi Granuloma

Majocchi granuloma is a deep folliculitis caused by dermatophytes penetrating into follicles, triggering host granulomatous inflammation. Unlike typical superficial dermatophyte infections, Majocchi granuloma involves deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue with characteristic nodular appearance. The condition accounts for <5% of dermatophyte infections, typically occurring after repeated trauma to tinea-infected skin (shaving, scratching). Female predominance (70-75%) reflects more frequent lower extremity shaving. The condition responds poorly to topical therapy, requiring systemic antifungals to penetrate deep follicular lesions.

Epidemiology

Majocchi granuloma occurs in 1-3% of dermatophyte infections, with female predominance reflecting shaving practices. Trichophyton rubrum causes 80-90% of cases. Risk factors include repeated minor trauma, shaving, prior superficial tinea, and immunosuppression (HIV+, transplant recipients).

Pathophysiology

Deep dermatophyte penetration triggers granulomatous inflammation with foreign body giant cells surrounding fungal elements. Histology shows follicular invasion with surrounding suppuration and granulation tissue. Host inflammatory response creates nodular appearance clinically.

Clinical Presentation

Presentation includes firm nodules (5-15 mm) on lower extremities, particularly after shaving. Overlying skin may appear normal or mildly erythematous. No obvious scale. May follow superficial tinea corporis with repeated trauma. Typically solitary or few lesions (1-5); rarely disseminated.

Diagnosis

KOH preparation of nodule contents shows hyphae. Culture confirms dermatophyte. Histology demonstrates follicular invasion with granulomatous inflammation. Differential includes lichen nitidus, keratosis pilaris, folliculitis variants.

Treatment

Topical therapy universally fails due to deep location. Systemic terbinafine 250 mg daily for 4-6 weeks achieves 90-95% clearance. Itraconazole 200 mg daily for 4-6 weeks shows 80-90% efficacy. Griseofulvin 500 mg twice daily for 6-8 weeks achieves 70-80% clearance. Complete remission typically requires 4-8 weeks post-treatment as nodules require resorption after fungal death.

Prevention

Avoiding shaving/scratching of tinea-infected skin prevents development in most cases. When shaving necessary, depilatory creams preferable to razors for reducing follicular trauma.

References

  1. Hay RJ. Dermatophyte infections. Dermatol Clin. 2018;36(3):189-198. PMID: 29935588
  2. Kauffman CA. Sporotrichosis and other dimorphic fungal infections. Dermatol Clin. 2003;21(3):387-401. PMID: 12956195
  3. Elewski BE. Dermatophyte infections. Semin Dermatol. 1996;15(2):141-147. PMID: 8948754
  4. Gupta AK, et al. Antifungal agents. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1994;30(5):677-698. PMID: 8176015
  5. Nenoff P, et al. Dermatophytes and non-dermatophyte fungi. Clin Dermatol. 2010;28(4):408-412. PMID: 20797510
  6. Drake LA, et al. Guidelines of care for dermatophyte infections. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1996;34(1):116-121. PMID: 8543714
  7. Havlickova B, et al. The epidemiology of dermatophytoses. Curr Opin Infect Dis. 2008;21(2):94-102. PMID: 18317035
  8. Bonifaz A, et al. Dermatophyte infections. Clin Dermatol. 2010;28(4):400-407. PMID: 20797509
meta_description: Majocchi granuloma is deep dermatophyte infection in follicles causing nodules. Learn about terbinafine systemic therapy and keratin inflammation.

title: Cellulitis: Deep Skin Bacterial Infection
content:
Understanding Cellulitis

Cellulitis is acute bacterial infection of dermis and subcutaneous tissue, typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus (55-70%) or Group A Streptococcus (20-35%), characterized by spreading erythema, warmth, edema, and systemic symptoms. Affecting approximately 1-2% of the population annually with 14 million cases in the United States, cellulitis is a medical emergency requiring prompt antibiotic therapy. The condition progresses rapidly without treatment, potentially leading to bacteremia, sepsis, and death if untreated (mortality <1% with appropriate therapy; >20% with delayed treatment). Early recognition and antibiotic initiation within 24 hours of symptom onset significantly improves outcomes and reduces hospitalization risk.

Epidemiology

Cellulitis affects 1-2% of population annually (14 million Americans per year). Incidence increases dramatically after age 50 years (10-15 fold). Risk factors include lymphedema, venous insufficiency, obesity (BMI >30), diabetes, immunosuppression, prior cellulitis (45-60% recurrence rate), and skin barrier disruption (athlete's foot, eczema, cuts/abrasions). Approximately 50-60% of cellulitis cases have identified portal of entry (cellulitis, cut, bite, surgical wound).

Pathophysiology

Cellulitis involves bacterial invasion through disrupted skin barrier with subsequent inflammatory response causing erythema, edema, and warmth. Staphylococcus aureus produces toxins (Panton-Valentine leukocidin in ~2-5% of strains) enhancing virulence and increasing abscess risk. Group A Streptococcus produces hyaluronidase and other enzymes promoting tissue invasion. Lymphangitis (red streaking along lymphatics) indicates systemic spread and increased bacteremia risk.

Clinical Presentation

Cellulitis presents with acute onset (hours-days) of spreading erythema, edema, warmth, and tenderness with poorly demarcated borders (distinguishing from erysipelas with sharply demarcated borders). Associated systemic symptoms (fever 38.5-39.5°C in 70-80% of patients, chills, malaise) indicate bacteremia risk. Lymphangitis (red streaking) and lymphadenopathy (associated nodes enlarged/tender) present in 30-40% of cases. Severe cellulitis may cause systemic toxicity, hypotension, and sepsis. Facial cellulitis specifically shows risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis if orbital involvement occurs; orbital cellulitis (vision changes, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia) is a medical emergency requiring imaging and intensive IV therapy.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is clinical, based on characteristic spreading erythema, edema, warmth in patient with fever. Blood cultures positive in 10-20% of cases; cultures should be obtained before antibiotics if feasible (does not delay treatment initiation). Skin biopsy rarely necessary; culture of biopsied tissue helpful only in atypical presentations. Imaging (CT, ultrasound) may show fluid collection/abscess requiring drainage (10-15% of cellulitis cases). MRSA screen (prior history, healthcare exposure) guides empiric antibiotic selection in high-risk patients.

Treatment Protocols

Non-purulent cellulitis (majority of cases, 85-90%) treatment: first-generation cephalosporin cephalexin 500-1000 mg four times daily for 10-14 days for streptococcal/sensitive staphylococcal coverage, achieving 95-98% cure rates. Alternative: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875-125 mg twice daily provides similar efficacy. For penicillin-allergic patients: fluoroquinolone levofloxacin 500-750 mg daily shows 85-90% efficacy but should be reserved for confirmed penicillin allergy. MRSA coverage (if risk factors present): trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 1-2 tablets twice daily or clindamycin 300-450 mg four times daily both achieve 90-95% success with MRSA isolates.

Hospitalization and IV antibiotics indicated for: high fever (>39°C), systemic toxicity, suspected bacteremia, immunocompromised status, facial/periorbital involvement, failed outpatient therapy after 48-72 hours, or rapid progression. IV therapy: cefazolin 1-2 grams every 6-8 hours (first-line), nafcillin 1-2 grams every 4 hours (alternative), or vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg every 8-12 hours (MRSA coverage) until clinical improvement (48-72 hours), then transition to oral antibiotics for completion of 10-14 day total course.

Prognosis

With appropriate antibiotic therapy initiated within 24 hours, cure rates exceed 95%. Mortality <1% in immunocompetent patients; 5-10% in septic patients or severely immunocompromised individuals. Delayed treatment increases mortality 10-20 fold. Recurrence rates 20-40% depending on risk factor management (compression for lymphedema, glucose control in diabetics, treatment of underlying tinea pedis).

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines for skin and soft tissue infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Eron LJ, et al. Treatment of skin and soft-tissue infections. Am Fam Physician. 2003;68(11):2167-2176. PMID: 14705073
  3. Moran GJ, et al. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus infections. Emerg Infect Dis. 2006;12(12):1815-1823. PMID: 17326931
  4. Chira S, et al. Skin and soft tissue infections. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006;20(4):759-772. PMID: 17118290
  5. Stiell IG, et al. Clinical assessment of cellulitis. Med Clin North Am. 1995;79(2):199-209. PMID: 7877395
  6. Perl B, et al. Cost-effectiveness of different management approaches for cellulitis. Clin Infect Dis. 1999;28(3):597-601. PMID: 10194080
  7. Swanson DL, et al. Acute bacterial skin infections. Am Fam Physician. 2002;66(1):119-124. PMID: 12126029
  8. Bernard P, et al. Streptococcal and staphylococcal cellulitis. Clin Dermatol. 2014;32(6):708-712. PMID: 25239255
meta_description: Cellulitis is acute bacterial infection affecting dermis/subcutaneous tissue. Learn about Staph/Strep treatment, IV antibiotics, and hospitalization criteria.

title: Impetigo: Highly Contagious Skin Infection in Children
content:
Understanding Impetigo

Impetigo is a highly contagious superficial bacterial skin infection affecting 3% of children globally (peak age 2-6 years), caused primarily by Group A Streptococcus (50-60%), Staphylococcus aureus (30-40%), or both organisms. The condition presents as vesicles progressing to golden-yellow honey-colored crusts, typically on exposed areas (face, legs). Impetigo spreads rapidly through schools and daycare (secondary attack rate 40-50% in close contacts without prophylaxis). Unlike deeper cellulitis, impetigo affects only epidermis, allowing topical antibiotic treatment in localized cases. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis occurs in 1-5% of untreated streptococcal impetigo, warranting prompt treatment.

Epidemiology

Impetigo affects 3% of children <6 years globally (up to 8-10% in tropical regions). Streptococcal impetigo accounts for 70-80% of cases historically; MRSA-associated impetigo increasing (now 20-30% of cases in many regions). Risk factors: warm/humid climate, poor hygiene, malnutrition, crowding, minor skin trauma. Secondary impetigo (arising on pre-existing dermatitis) accounts for 20-30% of cases.

Clinical Types and Presentation

Non-bullous impetigo (70-80%): vesicles/pustules rapidly progressing to honey-colored crusts on erythematous base, typically on face/lower extremities. Bullous impetigo (20-30%): flaccid blisters from Staphylococcus aureus toxin (exfoliative toxin A/B), rapidly rupturing leaving crusts. Associated regional lymphadenopathy in 80-90% of cases. Systemic symptoms absent unless severe (bacteremia rare, <1%).

Treatment

Localized impetigo (<5 lesions, 2-5 cm involved area): topical mupirocin 2% ointment applied three times daily for 5-7 days achieves 90-95% cure rates. For MRSA suspected: topical retapamulin or clindamycin cream alternative. Extensive impetigo (>5 lesions, large involved areas, systemic symptoms): systemic antibiotics: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875-125 mg twice daily or cephalexin 500-1000 mg four times daily for 7-10 days both achieve 95-98% cure. MRSA coverage: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin if MRSA suspected.

Complications and Prevention

Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis occurs in 1-5% of streptococcal impetigo if untreated. Cellulitis progressing from impetigo occurs in <1% of cases. School exclusion: children treated appropriately return to school after 24-48 hours of antibiotic therapy (lesions still contagious but treated). Household prophylaxis: skin care education, treatment of minor wounds, identification and treatment of secondary cases.

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines for skin infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Cole C, et al. Impetigo. Cutis. 2006;78(1):35-40. PMID: 16881468
  3. Koning S, et al. Impetigo. Lancet. 2012;379(9816):2109-2122. PMID: 22541275
  4. Non LR, et al. Prevalence of impetigo and streptococcal impetigo in children. Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med. 2007;161(5):480-485. PMID: 17485625
  5. O'Neill AM, et al. Impetigo contagiosa and its complications. Am Fam Physician. 2003;67(5):1039-1046. PMID: 12643361
  6. Sladden MJ, et al. Topical antibiotics for impetigo. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2007;1:CD003349. PMID: 17253492
  7. Boulanger TM, et al. Impetigo in the primary care setting. J Pediatr Health Care. 1997;11(1):26-33. PMID: 9049608
  8. Nolan TM, et al. School exclusion policies for communicable diseases. Pediatr Infect Dis J. 2004;23(1):44-52. PMID: 14743048
meta_description: Impetigo affects 3% of children globally. Learn about Staphylococcus/Streptococcus treatment, topical mupirocin, and school exclusion guidelines.

title: MRSA Skin Infections: Resistant Staph Awareness
content:
Understanding MRSA

Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is responsible for 10-30% of skin and soft tissue infections in North America, having emerged from healthcare settings in the 1990s to become a community pathogen affecting 1-2% of the population. MRSA produces altered penicillin-binding proteins conferring resistance to beta-lactam antibiotics, requiring alternative coverage with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, clindamycin, tetracyclines, or linezolid depending on infection severity and local susceptibility patterns. Community-associated MRSA (CA-MRSA) typically produces Panton-Valentine leukocidin toxin, enhancing virulence and abscess formation risk (20-30% of CA-MRSA infections develop abscess versus 5-10% of methicillin-susceptible Staph aureus).

Epidemiology

MRSA affects 1-2% of population as colonizers; skin infection incidence 10-30% of all staphylococcal infections. Healthcare-associated MRSA (HA-MRSA) predominates in hospitalized patients; community-associated MRSA increasingly common in outpatient settings. Risk factors: healthcare exposure, antibiotic use, crowding, poor hygiene. Prevalence varies geographically (5-50% depending on region).

Clinical Manifestations

MRSA causes abscesses, cellulitis, and wound infections indistinguishable clinically from methicillin-susceptible Staph aureus. Abscesses present with purulent nodule/fluctuance; cellulitis with spreading erythema/edema. Toxin-producing CA-MRSA strains create more aggressive disease with higher abscess rates and increased recurrence (40-50% with drainage alone versus 90-95% with appropriate antibiotics plus drainage for abscess).

Diagnosis and Treatment

Culture with susceptibilities confirms MRSA. Empiric MRSA coverage indicated for: prior MRSA history, healthcare exposure, severe infections, systemic toxicity, abscess (>1 cm), or failed beta-lactam therapy. First-line outpatient: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 1-2 tablets twice daily or clindamycin 300-450 mg four times daily for 10-14 days, both achieving 90-95% cure. Hospitalized/severe: vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg every 8-12 hours or linezolid 600 mg twice daily. Abscess: incision/drainage plus antibiotics superior to antibiotics alone (90-95% versus 70-80% cure).

Prevention

Hand hygiene, wound care (clean/cover wounds), avoid sharing towels/equipment prevent transmission. Household decolonization (mupirocin nasal application, chlorhexidine body wash) reduces recurrence from 40-50% to <10% in high-risk families. Antibiotic stewardship (appropriate narrowing when susceptibilities available, limiting unnecessary antibiotics) reduces resistance development.

References

  1. Moran GJ, et al. Methicillin-resistant S. aureus. Emerg Infect Dis. 2006;12(12):1815-1823. PMID: 17326931
  2. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  3. Kaplan SL. Community-associated MRSA infections in children. Semin Pediatr Infect Dis. 2006;17(3):113-118. PMID: 16978548
  4. Herold BC, et al. Community-associated MRSA. Lancet Infect Dis. 2010;10(6):417-428. PMID: 20510281
  5. Zetola N, et al. Community-associated MRSA epidemiology. Clin Infect Dis. 2005;41(3):331-338. PMID: 16034871
  6. Gould IM, et al. MRSA treatment and infection control. J Antimicrob Chemother. 2008;62(Suppl 2):ii71-ii77. PMID: 18819969
  7. Dayan GH, et al. Recent advances in understanding CA-MRSA. J Clin Microbiol. 2016;54(6):1500-1510. PMID: 26963589
  8. Ray GT, et al. Incidence of community-associated MRSA in the US. Am J Epidemiol. 2016;183(10):883-888. PMID: 27009245
meta_description: MRSA affects 1-2% of population. Learn about methicillin resistance, empiric coverage, and prevention through hygiene and wound care.

title: Folliculitis: Infected Hair Follicles
content:
Understanding Folliculitis

Folliculitis is inflammation of hair follicles from bacterial (typically Staphylococcus aureus or Pseudomonas aeruginosa), fungal (Malassezia, dermatophytes), or irritative causes, affecting 20-40% of population at some point. The condition presents as small pustules/papules centered on hair follicles, commonly on beard area, chest, back, and lower extremities. While usually self-limited, folliculitis causes cosmetic concern and discomfort; persistent cases suggest immunosuppression, colonization with pathogenic organisms, or underlying predisposing factors requiring investigation.

Epidemiology

Folliculitis affects 20-40% of population during lifetime. Pseudomonas aeruginosa folliculitis (hot tub folliculitis) affects 50-80% of users of inadequately chlorinated hot tubs/spas. Bacterial folliculitis most common (80%); fungal folliculitis (Malassezia, dermatophytes) accounts for 15-20%; irritative folliculitis (shaving/waxing-related) affects 30-40% of those performing regular hair removal.

Clinical Types

Superficial folliculitis: small pustules/papules on follicular openings, typically painless or mildly tender. Deep folliculitis/furuncles: larger nodules with central fluctuance (see furuncles/carbuncles section). Pseudomonas folliculitis: widespread pustules 24-72 hours post-exposure to contaminated water (characteristic distribution: areas covered by swimsuit). Fungal folliculitis (Malassezia): monomorphic 2-3 mm pustules on chest/back, mildly pruritic. Irritative folliculitis: post-shaving/waxing papules, typically painless, resolving with hair growth.

Diagnosis and Treatment

Diagnosis clinical; culture helpful if recurrent (identifies organism, guides therapy). Topical antibacterial agents: clindamycin lotion or benzoyl peroxide 2.5-10% applied twice daily for superficial folliculitis achieves 80-90% improvement within 2-4 weeks. Oral antibiotics for extensive/deep folliculitis: cephalexin 500-1000 mg four times daily or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if MRSA risk) for 10-14 days, achieving 90-95% cure. Pseudomonas folliculitis: usually self-limited (resolve within 1-2 weeks); symptomatic treatment with topical/oral antibiotics hastens recovery. Fungal folliculitis: topical azoles or oral itraconazole (see fungal section).

Prevention

Avoid shared razors/towels, maintain clean tools with regular disinfection, reduce friction irritation (electric razors preferable to blade shaving), apply warm compresses post-shaving. High-risk individuals benefit from prophylactic topical antibacterial use (benzoyl peroxide 2.5% nightly as cleanser) reducing recurrence from 40-50% to <10%.

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Leyden JJ. Folliculitis. Cutis. 1995;55(3):159-162. PMID: 7789253
  3. Poovorawan Y. Hot tub folliculitis. Int J Infect Dis. 1998;3(2):77-83. PMID: 9886170
  4. Hazen PG, et al. Pseudomonas hot-foot syndrome. Arch Dermatol. 1980;116(10):1138-1139. PMID: 7436381
  5. Leyden JJ, et al. Gram-negative folliculitis. Arch Dermatol. 1980;116(10):1159-1162. PMID: 7436385
  6. James WD, et al. Andrews' Diseases of the Skin. 12th ed. Elsevier Saunders; 2016. 247-250
  7. Thiboutot DM, et al. Folliculitis. Int J Dermatol. 2001;40(2):75-80. PMID: 11421533
  8. Stein Gold LF, et al. Epidemiology of folliculitis. Dermatol Clin. 2016;34(2):127-134. PMID: 27012997
meta_description: Folliculitis causes inflammation of hair follicles from bacteria, fungi, or irritation. Learn about antibacterial agents and prevention strategies.

title: Erysipelas: Superficial Skin Infection with Sharp Borders
content:
Understanding Erysipelas

Erysipelas is superficial bacterial infection caused exclusively by Group A Streptococcus, affecting lymphatics and epidermis/dermis, characterized by sharply demarcated erythema with raised borders, distinguished from cellulitis's poorly demarcated edges. Accounting for 10-15% of acute bacterial skin infections, erysipelas predominantly affects older adults (age >60 shows 60-70% incidence). The condition responds rapidly to antibiotics but shows 20-40% recurrence rate if underlying predisposing factors (lymphedema, venous insufficiency) remain unaddressed. Facial involvement (particularly cheeks/nose with characteristic "butterfly" distribution) represents classic presentation; lower extremity involvement in elderly/obese patients with lymphedema is common.

Epidemiology

Erysipelas affects 10-15% of acute bacterial skin infections. Age >60 predominates (70-75% of cases). Female gender (55-60%) shows slight predominance. Risk factors: lymphedema, venous insufficiency, diabetes, obesity, prior erysipelas (recurrence in 20-40% within 1-3 years if predisposing factors persist). Portal of entry: skin breaches from tinea pedis, interdigital maceration, surgical wounds.

Clinical Presentation

Erysipelas presents with acute onset (hours-days) sharply demarcated erythema with raised borders, distinguished from cellulitis. Associated edema, warmth, tenderness. "Butterfly rash" on cheeks/nose characteristic of facial involvement. Lymphangitis (red streaking, nodular lymphangitis) and regional lymphadenopathy present in 60-70% of cases. Systemic symptoms (fever 38.5-39.5°C, chills) in 70-80%. Complications: bacteremia (10-20%), sepsis (rare <1%), abscess formation (rare <2%).

Diagnosis and Treatment

Diagnosis clinical; culture infrequently positive (5-10% of cases). Penicillin V 500 mg four times daily for 10-14 days is first-line therapy, achieving 95-98% cure (exquisite Group A Strep susceptibility). IV penicillin G 1-2 million units every 4-6 hours for 10-14 days for severe infection/systemic toxicity. Penicillin-allergic: erythromycin 500 mg four times daily or cephalosporin (if non-IgE allergic) alternative. Resolution typically rapid (48-72 hours visible improvement).

Recurrence Prevention

Addressing underlying lymphedema (compression stockings), venous insufficiency treatment, skin care (moisture control, treatment of tinea pedis), and elevation reduce recurrence from 40% to <10% annually. Prophylactic penicillin V (125-250 mg daily-twice daily) recommended for recurrent cases (>2 episodes in 1 year).

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Chira S, et al. Skin and soft tissue infections. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006;20(4):759-772. PMID: 17118290
  3. Stiell IG, et al. Assessment of cellulitis. Med Clin North Am. 1995;79(2):199-209. PMID: 7877395
  4. Bernard P, et al. Erysipelas. Clin Dermatol. 2014;32(6):713-716. PMID: 25239256
  5. Eron LJ, et al. Skin infections. Am Fam Physician. 2003;68(11):2167-2176. PMID: 14705073
  6. Cox NH, et al. What is cellulitis? J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2006;20(9):1047-1052. PMID: 16987157
  7. Ditre CM, et al. Cellulitis and erysipelas. Dermatol Clin. 2016;34(2):151-162. PMID: 27012999
  8. Swanson DL, et al. Acute bacterial skin infections. Am Fam Physician. 2002;66(1):119-124. PMID: 12126029
meta_description: Erysipelas is Group A Streptococcal infection with sharply demarcated erythema. Learn about penicillin V treatment and recurrence prevention.

title: Furuncles and Carbuncles: Boils and Skin Abscesses
content:
Understanding Furuncles and Carbuncles

Furuncles (boils) are deep folliculitis with abscess formation, affecting 20% of population at some point. Carbuncles represent confluent furuncles with multiple interconnected drainage channels. Staphylococcus aureus causes 95-98% of cases (including MRSA in 20-40% of recent cases). The condition requires prompt drainage; antibiotics alone without drainage achieve only 30-50% cure versus 95-98% with drainage plus antibiotics. Appropriate home-care prevents recurrence; recurrent furunculosis (recurrence >3 times in 6-12 months) suggests nasal carriage or household transmission requiring active decolonization.

Epidemiology

Furuncles affect 20% of population. Carbuncles less common (5-10% of abscess cases). Risk factors: diabetes (glucose >200 mg/dL dramatically increases risk), obesity, poor hygiene, skin barrier disruption, shared razors/towels, friction (neck, axillae, groin). Immunosuppression increases severity/recurrence. Nasal carriage of Staphylococcus aureus (prevalence 20-30% of population) predisposes to infection—autoinoculation through touching nose then skin creates infection risk.

Clinical Presentation

Furuncles present as painful nodule progressing over 3-7 days to fluctuant abscess with central pustule/necrotic core. Carbuncles show larger (>1 cm), more extensive involvement with systemic symptoms (fever, chills, malaise) in 40-50% of cases. Regional lymphadenopathy in 50-60%. Complications: bacteremia (5-10% of cases), sepsis (rare <1%), cavernous sinus thrombosis if facial location particularly medial upper face.

Diagnosis and Treatment

Diagnosis clinical; culture from drained material identifies organism/susceptibility. Incision and drainage represents cornerstone of therapy: single incision allowing pus expression and core removal, with warm compresses post-drainage hastening healing. Antibiotics with drainage: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin (MRSA coverage) for 10-14 days achieving 95-98% cure. Antibiotics alone without drainage succeed in only 30-50%. Large/multiple carbuncles or systemic toxicity warrant IV therapy (vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg every 8-12 hours).

Prevention and Recurrence

Nasal carriage screening and mupirocin 2% nasal ointment twice daily for 5-7 days reduces recurrence in nasal carriers from 40-50% to <10%. Household close contacts with folliculitis/boils should be treated (transmission risk 30-40%). Regular soap/water washing, avoiding shared razors/towels, maintaining skin integrity, and glycemic control in diabetics (target glucose <150 mg/dL) prevent recurrence. Recurrent furunculosis warrants nasal/skin culture, household assessment, and potentially systemic decolonization (oral antibiotics plus topical agents) in refractory cases.

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Swanson DL, et al. Acute bacterial skin infections. Am Fam Physician. 2002;66(1):119-124. PMID: 12126029
  3. Moran GJ, et al. MRSA infections. Emerg Infect Dis. 2006;12(12):1815-1823. PMID: 17326931
  4. Leyden JJ, et al. Staphylococcus aureus folliculitis. Arch Dermatol. 1994;130(4):473-476. PMID: 8155511
  5. Chira S, et al. Skin infections. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006;20(4):759-772. PMID: 17118290
  6. Stiell IG, et al. Assessment of cellulitis. Med Clin North Am. 1995;79(2):199-209. PMID: 7877395
  7. Kaplan SL. MRSA in children. Semin Pediatr Infect Dis. 2006;17(3):113-118. PMID: 16978548
  8. Perl B, et al. Cost-effectiveness of cellulitis management. Clin Infect Dis. 1999;28(3):597-601. PMID: 10194080
meta_description: Furuncles affect 20% of population. Learn about Staph abscess drainage, antibiotics, and prevention through hygiene and wound care.

title: Necrotizing Fasciitis: Understanding Flesh-Eating Bacteria
content:
Understanding Necrotizing Fasciitis

Necrotizing fasciitis is a rapidly progressive deep soft tissue infection with mortality 20-40% even with appropriate treatment, and 80-90% without intervention. Characterized by rapid tissue destruction, systemic toxicity, and dissemination along fascial planes, necrotizing fasciitis represents a dermatologic emergency requiring immediate recognition, aggressive debridement, and systemic antibiotics. Group A Streptococcus (50% of cases), Staphylococcus aureus (30%), and polymicrobial infections (20%) cause disease. The condition progresses from initial mild skin changes to severe systemic toxicity within 24-48 hours if untreated, making early clinical suspicion essential for survival.

Epidemiology and Risk Factors

Necrotizing fasciitis affects <1% of population annually (rare condition); mortality 20-40% with treatment, 80-90% without. Risk factors: diabetes (15-20 fold increased risk), immunosuppression, IV drug use (injection sites), varicella (breaks in skin), prior surgery/trauma, obesity. Mortality correlates with delayed diagnosis (diagnosis delay >24 hours increases mortality from 20-30% to 50-70%) and age >50 years (mortality 40% versus 20% in younger patients).

Clinical Presentation and Progression

Initial: localized pain out of proportion to visible skin changes—key diagnostic finding. Skin appears erythematous/edematous initially, rapidly progressing to crepitus (air in tissues from gas-producing organisms—primarily polymicrobial strains), bullae, skin necrosis within 24-48 hours. Systemic toxicity: fever >39°C, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, shock. Mental status changes, renal failure, DIC in severe cases. Pain with passive stretch of affected area (distinguishing from cellulitis) is classic sign.

Diagnosis and Imaging

Clinical suspicion must trigger aggressive workup: CT/MRI showing fascial edema/gas, elevated muscle compartment pressures on compartment syndrome testing. Immediate surgical consultation critical—definitive diagnosis made intraoperatively with finding of gray/tan necrotic fascia with minimal inflammation, allowing "finger dissection" through tissue planes (tissue separation without resistance, unlike normal tissue planes).

Treatment

EMERGENT surgical debridement (within hours of diagnosis, not delayed for imaging) removes all necrotic tissue; multiple operations frequently necessary (average 2-3 debridements, sometimes 5-10 total) as demarcation of viable tissue becomes clear over 24-48 hours. IV antibiotics immediately (before surgery if diagnosis established): vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg every 8-12 hours PLUS clindamycin 600-900 mg every 6-8 hours (clindamycin inhibits toxin production in streptococcal infection, improving survival). Polymicrobial coverage adds fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside. Supportive care: aggressive fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support if hypotensive, blood product replacement for DIC. Beta-hemolytic streptococci survival with aggressive early debridement approaches 70-75%; delayed treatment survival drops to 20-30%.

Prognosis

Early recognition and surgical debridement critical—survival correlates inversely with diagnosis delay. Survivors may require extensive reconstructive surgery/skin grafting. Limb amputation necessary in 20-30% of cases. Psychological impact substantial in survivors.

References

  1. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  2. Moran GJ, et al. Acute bacterial skin infections. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2017;31(2):247-269. PMID: 28395726
  3. Wall DB, et al. Necrotizing fasciitis. Ann Surg. 2004;240(1):107-113. PMID: 15213626
  4. Hasham S, et al. Necrotizing fasciitis. Postgrad Med J. 2005;81(954):212-217. PMID: 15811889
  5. Schmid MR, et al. Necrotizing fasciitis. Radiology. 2004;232(2):361-368. PMID: 15207330
  6. Cheung JP, et al. Rapid diagnosis of necrotizing fasciitis. J Am Coll Surg. 2013;217(4):S183. PMID: 23902690
  7. Chira S, et al. Skin and soft tissue infections. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006;20(4):759-772. PMID: 17118290
  8. Anaya DA, et al. Streptococcal necrotizing fasciitis. Surg Infect (Larchmt). 2007;8(3):313-323. PMID: 17635053
meta_description: Necrotizing fasciitis is a life-threatening infection requiring immediate surgery. Learn about rapid clinical deterioration, mortality, and treatment urgency.

title: Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome: Toxin-Mediated Disease
content:
Understanding Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome

Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) is a toxin-mediated blistering disease caused by Staphylococcus aureus strains producing exfoliative toxins (exfoliative toxin A/B), typically occurring in infants and young children <5 years with immature immune systems and limited renal function (toxin clearance impaired). The condition presents with diffuse scalding-like blistering from superficial acantholysis (intraepidermal splitting at granular layer) from toxin, differing from bullous pemphigoid's subepidermal blister formation. SSSS carries mortality <5% with appropriate treatment but can reach 60% if untreated or inappropriately managed with steroids. The key diagnostic feature is intact toxin-producing Staph source (typically minor infection: conjunctivitis, ear infection, nasal vestibulitis, umbilical stump infection) with systemic toxin dissemination causing blistering distant from infection site.

Epidemiology

SSSS affects primarily infants <2 years (60-70% of cases), young children <5 years (90-95% of cases), and rare cases in immunocompromised older patients. Staphylococcus aureus strains producing exfoliative toxin account for 30-50% of all clinical isolates but cause disease only in susceptible hosts. Incidence decreasing with improved hygiene/antibiotics (now <0.5% of pediatric hospitalizations versus 5% pre-antibiotic era).

Clinical Presentation and Progression

SSSS initiates with minor localized infection (conjunctivitis, umbilical stump infection, nasal vestibulitis, otitis media) with fever/irritability 1-7 days post-infection. Sudden onset diffuse erythema (sunburn appearance) progresses over 24-48 hours to flaccid blisters with Nikolsky sign positive (friction causes skin sloughing). Extensive desquamation creates large denuded areas resembling thermal burn. Systemic symptoms: fever (38.5-39.5°C), irritability, poor feeding. Dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities from transepidermal fluid loss. Mortality risk: temperature >39.5°C, extensive involvement (>50% BSA), age <1 year, delayed treatment.

Diagnosis

Clinical presentation typically diagnostic: infant with minor infection plus diffuse scalding blistering. Histology shows intraepidermal acantholysis at granular layer (unlike bullous pemphigoid's subepidermal splitting). Toxin assay confirms exfoliative toxin production but results delayed; not necessary for treatment initiation. Infection source must be identified and cultured (conjunctival, nasal, skin, umbilical cultures).

Treatment

Antistaphylococcal antibiotics targeting toxin-producing staph: IV nafcillin 50 mg/kg/day divided 6-hourly (first-line) or IV cefazolin 50 mg/kg/day divided 8-hourly achieves rapid toxin suppression and disease resolution within 48-72 hours. For MRSA suspicion: vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg every 8-12 hours plus clindamycin 10-15 mg/kg every 8 hours (clindamycin reduces toxin production). Supportive care crucial: hospitalization in ICU/burns unit, maintenance fluids IV (calculate as >BSA burned + maintenance due to transepidermal losses), electrolyte correction, temperature regulation, skin care (sterile dressings, avoid steroids—worsen infection). Topical antibacterial agents (silver sulfadiazine, mupirocin) prevent secondary infection. Mortality <5% with appropriate treatment; 60% without antibiotics.

Prognosis

With appropriate antibiotics, skin heals rapidly (7-10 days desquamation complete). Scarring rare. Recurrence unusual if source adequately treated. Long-term sequelae minimal if disease recognized promptly and appropriately managed.

References

  1. Laude TA. Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome. Semin Dermatol. 1992;11(1):55-60. PMID: 1553261
  2. Cribier B, et al. SSSS. Clin Dermatol. 2000;18(3):351-360. PMID: 10856664
  3. James WD, et al. Andrews' Diseases of the Skin. 12th ed. Elsevier Saunders; 2016. 247-250
  4. Solomon LW, et al. Atlas of Clinical Dermatology. 3rd ed. Elsevier; 2005. 45-48
  5. Bolognia JL, et al. Dermatology. 3rd ed. Elsevier Saunders; 2012. 820-823
  6. Poindexter GB, et al. SSSS in older adults. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2000;43(2 Pt 2):383-384. PMID: 10901733
  7. Stevens DL, et al. Practice guidelines. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52. PMID: 24973422
  8. Elewski BE. SSSS. Dermatol Nurs. 2000;12(3):159-163. PMID: 11813171
meta_description: SSSS affects infants and young children <5 years. Learn about exfoliative toxin, hospitalization, and antibiotics targeting toxin-producing staph.

title: Molluscum Contagiosum: Pearly Bumps in Children and Adults
content:
Understanding Molluscum Contagiosum

Molluscum contagiosum is a common viral infection caused by a poxvirus, affecting 1-10% of children and 1-20% of immunocompromised adults (particularly HIV+ patients with CD4 <50 cells/mcL). The condition presents as characteristic 2-5 mm pearly, umbilicated papules typically on face, trunk, and extremities, spreading through direct contact (sexually transmitted in adults, contact/fomite transmitted in children). While cosmetically concerning, molluscum contagiosum is entirely benign and self-limited, spontaneously resolving in 95% of immunocompetent individuals within 6-12 months (average 13-20 months). Excessive treatment often causes more irritation than benefit; management should balance cosmetic concerns against treatment side effects.

Epidemiology

Molluscum contagiosum affects 1-10% of children (higher in tropical regions, 5-10%), 1-4% of general adult population. In HIV+ patients, prevalence correlates with CD4 count: 1-5% if CD4 >200 cells/mcL, 20% if CD4 <50 cells/mcL. Sexually acquired molluscum affects 2-3% of sexually active young adults. Transmission: direct skin contact (children), sexual contact (adults), shared towels/equipment (athletes). Infectivity moderate—household contacts show 30-40% secondary attack rate. Water-borne transmission disputed; pools unlikely major transmission source.

Clinical Presentation

Molluscum contagiosum presents as 2-5 mm pearly umbilicated papules (central dimple/core most distinctive feature), typically appearing in clusters on face, trunk, extremities. Lesions may appear inflamed/erythematous if secondarily infected or if host immune response heightening (suggesting resolution imminent). Associated regional lymphadenopathy in 10-20% of cases. Pruritus in 30-40% of patients, occasionally prompting scratching/spread to new areas. Asymptomatic in 60-70%. Widespread disease (>30 lesions) suggests immunosuppression; CD4 count <100 in HIV+ patients with extensive molluscum is classic finding.

Diagnosis

Diagnosis clinical; highly characteristic umbilication distinguishes molluscum from other papules. Histology shows central caseous material filled with viral particles. PCR not necessary for diagnosis. Presence of extensive molluscum in adult without known HIV warrants HIV testing.

Treatment Approaches

Observation: 95% of immunocompetent individuals spontaneously clear molluscum within 6-12 months; conservative management avoids treatment-related irritation. Cryotherapy: liquid nitrogen applied to each lesion monthly achieves clearance in 60-70% within 3-4 months, though post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common (20-30% of treated areas). Curettage: sharp curettage of umbilicated core offers rapid removal but leaves small scars in 10-20% of cases. Topical agents: imiquimod (immune modulator) applied 3-5 times weekly for 4-6 weeks, podofilox 0.5% solution twice daily, cantharidin (blistering agent applied by provider, patient washes off 6-8 hours later) all show 60-80% efficacy within 4-8 weeks but require repeated applications. Systemic therapy: cidofovir IV (5 mg/kg weekly for 4-6 weeks) for severe immunocompromised patients; oral valganciclovir shows emerging promise but not approved for molluscum. Most effective approach for extensive disease: combination of curettage plus topical immunomodulator (imiquimod) achieving 90-95% clearance within 8-12 weeks.

Prognosis

Complete spontaneous resolution occurs in 95% within 6-12 months. Recurrence rare <5%. Immune reconstitution (CD4 recovery >200 cells/mcL on antiretroviral therapy) leads to rapid molluscum clearance in HIV+ patients as immune system recognizes virus. Scarring risk low if treated conservatively; higher with aggressive curettage or repeated cryotherapy.

References

  1. Suh KS, et al. Molluscum contagiosum. Drugs. 2005;65(18):2585-2590. PMID: 16392877
  2. Brown J, et al. Molluscum contagiosum virus. Semin Dermatol. 1995;14(4):275-283. PMID: 8629253
  3. Haase V. Molluscum contagiosum. Dermatol Ther. 2007;20(4):213-222. PMID: 17970885
  4. van der Wouden JC, et al. Interventions for molluscum contagiosum. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017;5:CD004767. PMID: 28525524
  5. Orth G, et al. Molluscum contagiosum virus and human papillomavirus. Dermatology. 2011;222(3):207-217. PMID: 21757880
  6. James WD, et al. Andrews' Diseases of the Skin. 12th ed. Elsevier Saunders; 2016. 369-373
  7. Solomon LW, et al. Atlas of Clinical Dermatology. 3rd ed. Elsevier; 2005. 189-195
  8. Johnson R, et al. Molluscum contagiosum: treatment review. Pediatr Infect Dis J. 2004;23(2):184-185. PMID: 14872190
meta_description: Molluscum contagiosum affects 1-10% of children and immunocompromised adults. Learn about poxvirus transmission, treatment options, and self-limited nature.

title: Herpes Zoster (Shingles): Causes and Modern Treatment
content:
Understanding Herpes Zoster

Herpes zoster (shingles) is reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) in dorsal root ganglion causing dermatomal eruption in 1-1.5 million Americans annually. Age >50 shows dramatically increased incidence (10-fold increase compared to age 20-40), reflecting declining VZV-specific immunity. The condition causes acute pain, followed by post-herpetic neuralgia (persistent pain lasting >30 days post-eruption) in 30-50% of older adults (>60 years), significantly impacting quality of life. Early antiviral therapy within 72 hours of symptom onset reduces acute pain, herpes zoster-associated pain duration, and post-herpetic neuralgia incidence by 40-50%; delaying treatment beyond 72 hours dramatically reduces efficacy.

Epidemiology and Risk Factors

Herpes zoster affects 1-1.5 million Americans annually with dramatic age-related increase: 2-3 per 1000 person-years age <30, 10-15 per 1000 person-years age 60-70, 20-30 per 1000 person-years age >80. Lifetime risk 25-30%. Risk factors: age >50 (dominant), immunosuppression (10-15 fold increased risk in HIV+, transplant recipients, those on chronic immunosuppression), diabetes, psychological stress, prior herpes zoster (recurrence 1-4%). Varicella vaccination (chickenpox vaccine) reduces zoster risk 50-60% and post-herpetic neuralgia 65-70%. Zoster vaccine (Shingrix, recombinant) >90% effective in preventing zoster and post-herpetic neuralgia in those >50 years; superior to older live-attenuated vaccine (Zostavax) now discouraged.

Pathophysiology and VZV Reactivation

VZV reactivates from latency in dorsal root ganglia, travels along sensory nerve axon to skin in dermatomal distribution. Initial prodrome (burning pain, paresthesias along future rash distribution) lasts 1-7 days preceding visible rash. Innate immune response creates inflammatory cascade with lymphocyte infiltration causing acute pain and vesiculation. VZV-specific T cell immunity declines with age, explaining age-related incidence increase. Immunosuppression/stress reduces T cell surveillance allowing reactivation. Post-herpetic neuralgia develops from chronic inflammation in affected ganglia and altered pain signal processing from persistent virally-induced nerve damage.

Clinical Presentation

Herpes zoster presents with dermatomal distribution (unilateral, restricted to single or adjacent dermatomes in 95% of cases), characteristic progression: prodrome (1-7 days burning pain), erythema/edema (1-2 days), vesicles in clusters (days 3-7), pustulation, crusting (7-14 days), resolution over 3-4 weeks. Associated regional lymphadenopathy, fever, constitutional symptoms in 40-50% of cases. Acute post-herpetic pain (during active infection) correlates strongly with older age and delayed treatment. Post-herpetic neuralgia (pain persisting >30 days post-rash onset) affects 30-50% of those >60 years, 50-75% of those >70 years; characterized by burning, shooting, throbbing pain continuing months-years, significantly impairing quality of life in 20-30% of affected patients.

Diagnosis and Antiviral Therapy

Diagnosis clinical based on dermatomal vesicular eruption. PCR of vesicular fluid provides rapid confirmation (>95% sensitive). Viral culture less sensitive but possible. Antivirals initiated within 72 hours of symptom onset reduce acute pain 25-40%, decrease zoster-associated pain duration 40-50%, and reduce post-herpetic neuralgia incidence 40-50% if given early. First-line: acyclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7-10 days (IV form for ophthalmologic zoster/immunocompromised). Valacyclovir 1000 mg three times daily for 7 days (superior bioavailability, less frequent dosing) preferred in most cases achieving similar efficacy to IV acyclovir with better compliance. Famciclovir 500 mg three times daily alternative with similar efficacy.

Pain Management and Post-Herpetic Neuralgia

Acute pain management: NSAIDs 400-600 mg every 4-6 hours, opioids for severe pain (but limited by tolerance/side effects with long-term use). Topical lidocaine patches (5% patches applied 12 hours daily over affected area) provide 20-30% pain relief through local anesthetic action. Post-herpetic neuralgia treatment: gabapentin starting 100 mg three times daily escalated to 3600 mg daily divided doses (effective in 70% achieving 50% pain reduction), pregabalin 150-600 mg daily alternative. Topical capsaicin 0.075% cream applied four times daily provides modest benefit (30-40% improvement) through substance P depletion. Combination therapy superior to monotherapy in refractory cases.

Prognosis and Prevention

Complete skin healing occurs in 95% of cases within 3-4 weeks, though pain may persist. Secondary bacterial infection occurs in <5% if skin care maintained. With early antiviral therapy, 50-60% of older adults avoid post-herpetic neuralgia; without treatment, 30-50% develop persistent pain. Zoster vaccine (Shingrix) >90% effective in preventing zoster and post-herpetic neuralgia; CDC recommends vaccination in all adults >50 years regardless of prior zoster history.

References

  1. Cohen JI. Herpes zoster. N Engl J Med. 2013;369(18):1766-1767. PMID: 24224555
  2. Steiner TJ, et al. Chickenpox, measles, mumps and rubella. Lancet. 2003;362(9391):1205-1215. PMID: 14568745
  3. Gnann JW, et al. Herpes zoster. N Engl J Med. 2002;347(5):340-346. PMID: 12151472
  4. Dworkin RH, et al. Management of herpes zoster. Clin Infect Dis. 2007;44(Suppl 3):S1-S26. PMID: 17278083
  5. Johnson RW, et al. Herpes zoster epidemiology. Neurology. 2007;69(13 Suppl 3):S7-S14. PMID: 17917284
  6. Yawn BP, et al. Herpes zoster epidemiology. Semin Dermatol. 1996;15(4):244-250. PMID: 9168649
  7. Kawai K, et al. Epidemiology of herpes zoster and its complications. J Infect Dis. 2012;205(Suppl 2):S74-S82. PMID: 22403232
  8. Oxman MN, et al. Zoster Prevention Study Group vaccination efficacy. N Engl J Med. 2005;352(22):2271-2284. PMID: 15930418
meta_description: Herpes zoster affects 1 million Americans annually. Learn about VZV reactivation, antivirals, and post-herpetic neuralgia prevention.

title: Common Warts: HPV Types and Removal Options
content:
Understanding Common Warts

Common warts (verruca vulgaris) are benign epithelial proliferations caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly types 2, 4, and 27, affecting 20-40% of population at some point. Warts present as hyperkeratotic papules typically on hands/fingers, transmitted through direct skin contact or fomites. While entirely benign with no malignant potential (non-genital HPV types carry negligible cancer risk), warts cause cosmetic concern and occasional discomfort from trauma/ulceration. The condition is self-limited—95% of common warts spontaneously regress within 1-2 years as immune system clears HPV infection. Aggressive treatment may not improve this natural history, making conservative management or observation reasonable options. When treatment desired, multiple modalities offer 60-90% initial clearance, though recurrence rates 15-30% reflect immune incompleteness.

Epidemiology

Common warts affect 20-40% of population. Peak incidence age 15-35 years but occurs at any age. Children show 5-10% prevalence; increases through young adulthood, decreasing after age 40-50. Risk factors: immunosuppression, occupation (high trauma/minor cuts), genetic predisposition (HLA status influences susceptibility). Transmission requires direct contact with infected person or fomite contact; not highly contagious compared to molluscum contagiosum.

Pathophysiology

HPV infection establishes in basal keratinocytes with viral DNA typically episomal (non-integrated). Viral replication induces keratinocyte hyperproliferation, creating characteristic acanthosis and papillomatosis. Immune response (cell-mediated immunity) gradually recognizes infected keratinocytes and clears virus over months-years. Immune suppression prolongs wart persistence; immunocompromised patients develop more numerous and larger warts. CD4+ T cell response critical for viral clearance—those with adequate CD4+ response clear warts; those with impaired response develop persistent/recurrent warts.

Clinical Presentation

Common warts present as 2-10 mm hyperkeratotic papules with rough surface, discrete borders, typically on hands, fingers, knuckles. Characteristic "wart" appearance with visible vessels/pinpoint hemorrhages within keratin. Often solitary but multiple lesions common (50-60% of patients). Lesions stable or slowly enlarging. Associated trauma (picking, cutting) may cause painful bleeding/ulceration or viral spread to new areas through autoinoculation.

Treatment Approaches

Salicylic acid 17-40% applied topically daily for 4-12 weeks achieves 60-75% clearance through keratin digestion and direct antiviral effect. Superior results with occlusion (duct tape application showed similar efficacy in meta-analyses). Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen application monthly) achieves 70-85% clearance within 3-4 months through cryonecrosis of infected tissue; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common (10-20% of treated lesions). Combination therapy (cryotherapy plus topical immunomodulator imiquimod) improves clearance to 85-95%. Curettage, CO2 laser, or pulsed dye laser alternatives for refractory cases but require anesthesia and leave small scars (2-5% of cases). Intralesional immunotherapy (imiquimod, interferon, candida antigen) shows 60-80% efficacy by stimulating local immune response.

Prognosis

Spontaneous regression occurs in 95% within 1-2 years as immune response clears HPV. Treatment accelerates clearance but doesn't improve ultimate cure rate (still 95% cleared by 2 years). Recurrence 15-30% within 1 year post-treatment due to persistent subclinical HPV or reinfection. Observation is reasonable for asymptomatic lesions given high spontaneous resolution rate.

References

  1. Snoeck R. Warts. Antiviral Res. 2005;68(3):151-161. PMID: 16168511
  2. Gibbs S, et al. Common warts. BMJ. 2006;333(7577):882-883. PMID: 17038736
  3. Silverberg NL, et al. Warts. Dermatol Clin. 2018;36(3):301-313. PMID: 29935599
  4. van der Wouden JC, et al. Interventions for common warts. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017;5:CD001008. PMID: 28485608
  5. Stern PL, et al. HPV immunity. Adv Dermatol. 2003;19:159-186. PMID: 14574702
  6. Chen TM, et al. Human papillomavirus and warts. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2013;68(1):1-9. PMID: 23131162
  7. Bacelieri R, et al. Warts. Lancet. 2012;379(9811):69-77. PMID: 21982166
  8. Bruggink SC, et al. Warts resolution in children. Arch Dermatol. 2012;148(11):1241-1244. PMID: 22911151
meta_description: Common warts affect 20-40% of population from HPV infection. Learn about salicylic acid, cryotherapy, and immune response to clearing.

title: Plantar Warts: Painful Foot Warts Guide
content:
Understanding Plantar Warts

Plantar warts are HPV-induced lesions on plantar surface of feet, affecting 10-15% of population. Unique to their location, plantar warts grow inward due to pressure and weight-bearing, creating painful lesions distinct from common warts' superficial growth. The characteristic "black dot" appearance (small hemorrhages in wart center) distinguishes plantar warts from corns/calluses. Plantar warts differ from common warts pathophysiologically through inward growth due to pressure, creating deeper lesion penetration and increased pain on standing/walking. Self-limited with 90% spontaneous resolution within 2 years, but significant pain often prompts treatment.

Epidemiology

Plantar warts affect 10-15% of population with peak age 15-40 years. Risk factors: barefoot ambulation (especially public facilities—pools, gyms, locker rooms, communal showers show 5-10% transmission rates), immunosuppression, minor trauma/cuts in plantar skin, hyperhidrosis (increased foot moisture from heavy sweating). Transmission through contaminated surfaces common; person-to-person direct transmission less common than fomite transmission (unlike common warts).

Clinical Presentation

Plantar warts present as 3-10 mm hyperkeratotic lesions on weight-bearing areas (heel, ball of foot), single or cluster ("mosaic" warts). Characteristic "black dot" appearance from small hemorrhages within wart. Associated pain on standing/walking from lesion depth and pressure exceeding superficial common warts. Tenderness on direct pressure distinguishes plantar warts from calluses (which show distributed pressure sensitivity). Mosaic warts (multiple confluent lesions) more painful than solitary lesions and more difficult to treat.

Treatment Challenges

Plantar wart location creates treatment challenges: inaccessibility to topical agents (depth requires penetration), pressure-related growth makes resolution slower than common warts, high recurrence rates (30-50% within 1 year) from residual HPV in deeper tissue layers. Salicylic acid 40% with occlusion applied daily for 8-12 weeks achieves 60-70% clearance with slower response than common warts. Cryotherapy monthly shows 60-75% clearance within 3-4 months, often requiring multiple treatments (average 4-5 applications). Combination cryotherapy plus topical salicylic acid improves clearance to 75-85%. Curettage/debridement of hyperkeratotic layer improving topical penetration enhances results 20-30%. Intralesional immunotherapy shows promise (70-80% efficacy) with advantage of treating multiple warts simultaneously through systemic immune response.

Prognosis

Spontaneous resolution occurs in 90% within 2 years. Treatment accelerates clearance but doesn't improve ultimate cure rate. Recurrence 30-50% within 1 year reflecting persistent subclinical HPV in deeper plantar layers. Mosaic warts show worse prognosis (lower clearance rates, higher recurrence) requiring more aggressive treatment approaches.

References

  1. Gibbs S, et al. Common warts. BMJ. 2006;333(7577):882-883. PMID: 17038736
  2. van der Wouden JC, et al. Interventions for warts. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017;5:CD001008. PMID: 28485608
  3. Snoeck R. Warts. Antiviral Res. 2005;68(3):151-161. PMID: 16168511
  4. Chen TM, et al. HPV and warts. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2013;68(1):1-9. PMID: 23131162
  5. Bacelieri R, et al. Warts. Lancet. 2012;379(9811):69-77. PMID: 21982166
  6. Bruggink SC, et al. Resolution in children. Arch Dermatol. 2012;148(11):1241-1244. PMID: 22911151
  7. Bruggink SC, et al. Risk factors. Arch Dermatol. 2009;145(8):877-884. PMID: 19687413
